Enter DON PEDRO

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
What secret hath held you here that you followed not
to Leonato’s?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: I would your grace would constrain me to tell.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: I charge thee on thy allegiance.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: I charge thee on thy allegiance.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
You hear, Count Claudio? I can be secret as a dumb man,
I would have you think so, but on my allegiance—mark
you this, on my allegiance— [to DON PEDRO] he is in love.
With who? Now, that is your Grace’s part. Mark how
short his answer is: with Hero, Leonato’s short
daughter.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: If this were so, so were it uttered.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Like the old tale, my lord: “It is not so nor ’twas
not so but, indeed, God forbid it should be so.”

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Like the old tale, my lord: “It is not so nor ’twas
not so but, indeed, God forbid it should be so.”

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
If my passion change not shortly, God forbid it should
be otherwise.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Amen, if you love her, for the lady is very well
worthy.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Amen, if you love her, for the lady is very well
worthy.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: You speak this to fetch me in, my lord.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: By my troth, I speak my thought.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: And, in faith, my lord, I spoke mine.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: And by my two faiths and troths, my lord, I spoke mine.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: That I love her, I feel.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: That she is worthy, I know.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
That I neither feel how she should be loved nor know
how she should be worthy is the opinion that fire cannot
melt out of me. I will die in it at the stake.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
That I neither feel how she should be loved nor know
how she should be worthy is the opinion that fire cannot
melt out of me. I will die in it at the stake.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
That I neither feel how she should be loved nor know
how she should be worthy is the opinion that fire cannot
melt out of me. I will die in it at the stake.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Thou wast ever an obstinate heretic in the despite of
beauty.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
And never could maintain his part but in the force of
his will.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
That a woman conceived me, I thank her. That she
brought me up, I likewise give her most humble thanks.
But that I will have a recheat winded in my forehead or
hang my bugle in an invisible baldrick, all women shall
pardon me. Because I will not do them the wrong to
mistrust any, I will do myself the right to trust none.
And the fine is, for the which I may go the finer, I will
live a bachelor.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: I shall see thee, ere I die, look pale with love.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
With anger, with sickness, or with hunger, my lord,
not with love. Prove that ever I lose more blood with
love than I will get again with drinking, pick out mine
eyes with a ballad-maker’s pen and hang me up at the
door of a brothel house for the sign of blind Cupid.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, if ever thou dost fall from this faith, thou wilt
prove a notable argument.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
If I do, hang me in a bottle like a cat and shoot at
me, and he that hits me, let him be clapped on the
shoulder and called Adam.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
If I do, hang me in a bottle like a cat and shoot at
me, and he that hits me, let him be clapped on the
shoulder and called Adam.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, as time shall try. In time the savage bull doth
bear the yoke.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, as time shall try. In time the savage bull doth
bear the yoke.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
The savage bull may, but if ever the sensible Benedick
bear it, pluck off the bull’s horns and set them in my
forehead, and let me be vilely painted, and in such
great letters as they write “Here is good horse to hire”
let them signify under my sign “Here you may see
Benedick the married man.”

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: If this should ever happen, thou wouldst be horn-mad.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Nay, if Cupid have not spent all his quiver in Venice,
thou wilt quake for this shortly.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: I look for an earthquake too, then.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, you temporize with the hours. In the meantime,
good Signior Benedick, repair to Leonato’s. Commend me
to him and tell him I will not fail him at supper, for
indeed he hath made great preparation.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, you temporize with the hours. In the meantime,
good Signior Benedick, repair to Leonato’s. Commend me

indeed he hath made great preparation.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I have almost matter enough in me for such an
embassage, and so I commit you—

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I have almost matter enough in me for such an
embassage, and so I commit you—

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: To the tuition of God. From my house, if I had it—

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: To the tuition of God. From my house, if I had it—

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: The sixth of July. Your loving friend, Benedick.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: The sixth of July. Your loving friend, Benedick.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Nay, mock not, mock not. The body of your discourse
is sometimes guarded with fragments and the guards are
but slightly basted on neither. Ere you flout old ends
any further, examine your conscience. And so I leave
you.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: My liege, your highness now may do me good.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
My love is thine to teach. Teach it but how,
And thou shalt see how apt it is to learn
Any hard lesson that may do thee good.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
My love is thine to teach. Teach it but how,
And thou shalt see how apt it is to learn
Any hard lesson that may do thee good.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Hath Leonato any son, my lord?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
No child but Hero; she’s his only heir.
Dost thou affect her, Claudio?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
No child but Hero; she’s his only heir.
Dost thou affect her, Claudio?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
O, my lord,
When you went onward on this ended action,
I looked upon her with a soldier’s eye,
That liked but had a rougher task in hand
Than to drive liking to the name of love.
But now I am returned and that war thoughts
Have left their places vacant, in their rooms
Come thronging soft and delicate desires,
All prompting me how fair young Hero is,
Saying I liked her ere I went to wars.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Thou wilt be like a lover presently
And tire the hearer with a book of words.
If thou dost love fair Hero, cherish it,
And I will break with her and with her father,
And thou shalt have her. Was ’t not to this end
That thou began’st to twist so fine a story?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
How sweetly you do minister to love,
That know love’s grief by his complexion!
But lest my liking might too sudden seem,
I would have salved it with a longer treatise.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
What need the bridge much broader than the flood?
The fairest grant is the necessity.
Look what will serve is fit. 'Tis once, thou lovest,
And I will fit thee with the remedy.
I know we shall have reveling tonight.
I will assume thy part in some disguise
And tell fair Hero I am Claudio,
And in her bosom I’ll unclasp my heart
And take her hearing prisoner with the force
And strong encounter of my amorous tale.
Then after to her father will I break,
And the conclusion is, she shall be thine.
In practice let us put it presently.

Enter LEONATO and ANTONIO

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
How now, brother, where is my cousin, your son? Hath
he provided this music?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
How now, brother, where is my cousin, your son? Hath
he provided this music?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
How now, brother, where is my cousin, your son? Hath
he provided this music?

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
He is very busy about it. But, brother, I can tell you
strange news that you yet dreamt not of.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
He is very busy about it. But, brother, I can tell you
strange news that you yet dreamt not of.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Are they good?

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
As the events stamps them, but they have a good
cover; they show well outward. The Prince and Count
Claudio, walking in a thick-pleached alley in mine
orchard, were thus much overheard by a man of mine: the
Prince discovered to Claudio that he loved my niece your

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Hath the fellow any wit that told you this?

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
A good sharp fellow. I will send for him, and question
him yourself.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: LEONATO

No, no, we will hold it as a dream till it appear

peradventure this be true. Go you and tell her of it.

Enter ANTONIO ’s son, with a musician and attendants

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Cousins, you know what you have to do.—O, I cry
you mercy, friend. Go you with me and I will use your
skill.—Good cousin, have a care this busy time.

Enter DON JOHN and CONRAD

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:
What the goodyear, my lord, why are you thus out
of measure sad?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
There is no measure in the occasion that breeds.
Therefore the sadness is without limit.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
There is no measure in the occasion that breeds.
Therefore the sadness is without limit.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: You should hear reason.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: And when I have heard it, what blessing brings it?

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: If not a present remedy, at least a patient sufferance.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
I wonder that thou, being, as thou sayst thou art,
born under Saturn, goest about to apply a moral medicine
to a mortifying mischief. I cannot hide what I am. I
must be sad when I have cause and smile at no man’s
jests, eat when I have stomach and wait for no man’s
leisure, sleep when I am drowsy and tend on no man’s
business, laugh when I am merry and claw no man in his
humor.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:
Yea, but you must not make the full show of this till
you may do it without controlment. You have of late
stood out against your brother, and he hath ta'en you
newly into his grace, where it is impossible you should
take true root but by the fair weather that you make
yourself. It is needful that you frame the season for
your own harvest.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
I had rather be a canker in a hedge than a rose in his
grace, and it better fits my blood to be disdained of
all than to fashion a carriage to rob love from any. In
this, though I cannot be said to be a flattering honest
man, it must not be denied but I am a plain-dealing
villain. I am trusted with a muzzle and enfranchised
with a clog; therefore I have decreed not to sing in my
cage. If I had my mouth, I would bite; if I had my
liberty, I would do my liking. In the meantime, let me
be that I am, and seek not to alter me.

be that I am, and seek not to alter me.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Can you make no use of your discontent?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
I make all use of it, for I use it only. Who comes
here?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
I make all use of it, for I use it only. Who comes
here?
Enter BORACHIO

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: What news, Borachio?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
I came yonder from a great supper. The Prince
your brother is royally entertained by Leonato, and I
can give you intelligence of an intended marriage.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
I came yonder from a great supper. The Prince
your brother is royally entertained by Leonato, and I
can give you intelligence of an intended marriage.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
I came yonder from a great supper. The Prince
your brother is royally entertained by Leonato, and I
can give you intelligence of an intended marriage.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Will it serve for any model to build mischief on? What
is he for a fool that betroths himself to unquietness?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Will it serve for any model to build mischief on? What
is he for a fool that betroths himself to unquietness?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Marry, it is your brother’s right hand.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: Who? The most exquisite Claudio?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: Who? The most exquisite Claudio?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Even he.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: A proper squire. And who, and who? Which way looks he?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: A proper squire. And who, and who? Which way looks he?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: A proper squire. And who, and who? Which way looks he?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Marry, on Hero, the daughter and heir of Leonato.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: A very forward March-chick! How came you to this?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: A very forward March-chick! How came you to this?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Being entertained for a perfumer, as I was smoking a
musty room, comes me the Prince and Claudio, hand in
hand, in sad conference. I whipped me behind the arras,
and there heard it agreed upon that the Prince should
woo Hero for himself, and having obtained her, give her
to Count Claudio.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Come, come, let us thither. This may prove food to
my displeasure. That young start-up hath all the glory
of my overthrow. If I can cross him any way, I bless
myself every way. You are both sure, and will assist me?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: To the death, my lord.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Let us to the great supper. Their cheer is the greater
that I am subdued. Would the cook were o' my mind! Shall
we go prove what’s to be done?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:

that I am subdued. Would the cook were o' my mind! Shall
we go prove what’s to be done?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: We’ll wait upon your lordship.
Exeunt
Enter LEONATO, ANTONIO, HERO, BEATRICE, URSULA and MARGARET

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Was not Count John here at supper?

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: I saw him not.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
How tartly that gentleman looks! I never can see him
but I am heartburned an hour after.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
How tartly that gentleman looks! I never can see him
but I am heartburned an hour after.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: He is of a very melancholy disposition.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
He were an excellent man that were made just in
the midway between him and Benedick. The one is too like
an image and says nothing, and the other too like my
lady’s eldest son, evermore tattling.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
He were an excellent man that were made just in
the midway between him and Benedick. The one is too like
an image and says nothing, and the other too like my
lady’s eldest son, evermore tattling.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Then half Signor Benedick’s tongue in Count
John’s mouth, and half Count John’s melancholy in
Signor Benedick’s face—

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
With a good leg and a good foot, uncle, and money
enough in his purse, such a man would win any woman in
the world, if he could get her goodwill.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
By my troth, niece, thou wilt never get thee a husband
if thou be so shrewd of thy tongue.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: In faith, she’s too curst.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Too curst is more than curst. I shall lessen God’s
sending that way, for it is said, “God sends a curst cow
short horns,” but to a cow too curst, he sends none.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Too curst is more than curst. I shall lessen God’s
sending that way, for it is said, “God sends a curst cow
short horns,” but to a cow too curst, he sends none.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Too curst is more than curst. I shall lessen God’s
sending that way, for it is said, “God sends a curst cow
short horns,” but to a cow too curst, he sends none.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: So, by being too curst, God will send you no horns.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Just, if he send me no husband, for the which blessing
I am at him upon my knees every morning and evening.
Lord, I could not endure a husband with a beard on his
face! I had rather lie in the woolen.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: You may light on a husband that hath no beard.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
What should I do with him? Dress him in my apparel
and make him my waiting gentlewoman? He that hath a
beard is more than a youth, and he that hath no beard is
less than a man; and he that is more than a youth is
not for me, and he that is less than a man, I am not for
him. Therefore I will even take sixpence in earnest of
the bearherd, and lead his apes into hell.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Well then, go you into hell?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
No, but to the gate, and there will the devil meet me
like an old cuckold with horns on his head, and say,
“Get you to heaven, Beatrice, get you to heaven; here’s
no place for you maids.” So deliver I up my apes and
away to Saint Peter. For the heavens, he shows me where
the bachelors sit, and there live we as merry as the day
is long.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
[to HERO] Well, niece, I trust you will be ruled by
your father.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Yes, faith, it is my cousin’s duty to make curtsy and
say, “Father, as it please you.” But yet for all that,
cousin, let him be a handsome fellow, or else make
another curtsy and say, “Father, as it please me.”

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
[to HERO] Daughter, I hope to see you one day fitted
with a husband.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Not till God make men of some other metal than earth.
Would it not grieve a woman to be overmastered with
a piece of valiant dust? To make an account of her life
to a clod of wayward marl? No, uncle, I’ll none. Adam’s
sons are my brethren, and truly I hold it a sin to match
in my kindred.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
[to HERO] Daughter, remember what I told you. If
the Prince do solicit you in that kind, you know your
answer.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
[to HERO] Daughter, remember what I told you. If
the Prince do solicit you in that kind, you know your
answer.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
The fault will be in the music, cousin, if you be not
wooed in good time. If the Prince be too important, tell
him there is measure in everything, and so dance out
the answer. For hear me, Hero, wooing, wedding, and
repenting is as a Scotch jig, a measure, and a
cinquepace. The first suit is hot and hasty like a
Scotch jig, and full as fantastical; the wedding,
mannerly modest as a measure, full of state
and ancientry; and then comes repentance, and with his
bad legs falls into the cinquepace faster and faster
till he sink into his grave.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Cousin, you apprehend passing shrewdly.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I have a good eye, uncle. I can see a church by
daylight.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I have a good eye, uncle. I can see a church by
daylight.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: The revelers are entering, brother. Make good room.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: The revelers are entering, brother. Make good room.
Enter DON PEDRO, CLAUDIO, BENEDICK, BALTHASAR, DON JOHN, BORACHIO, MARGARET, URSULA and others, masked
Enter DON PEDRO, CLAUDIO, BENEDICK, BALTHASAR, DON JOHN, BORACHIO, MARGARET, URSULA and others, masked

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Lady, will you walk a bout with your friend?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Lady, will you walk a bout with your friend?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
So you walk softly, and look sweetly, and say nothing,
I am yours for the walk, and especially when I walk
away.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: With me in your company?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: I may say so when I please.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: And when please you to say so?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
When I like your favor, for God defend the lute should
be like the case!

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: My visor is Philemon’s roof; within the house is Jove.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Why, then, your visor should be thatched.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Speak low if you speak love.
They move aside. BALTHASAR and MARGARET move forward
They move aside. BALTHASAR and MARGARET move forward

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: Well, I would you did like me.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
So would not I for your own sake, for I have many ill
qualities.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
So would not I for your own sake, for I have many ill
qualities.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: Which is one?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: I say my prayers aloud.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: I love you the better; the hearers may cry “Amen.”

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: I love you the better; the hearers may cry “Amen.”

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: God match me with a good dancer!

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: Amen.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: Amen.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
And God keep him out of my sight when the dance is
done! Answer, clerk.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
And God keep him out of my sight when the dance is
done! Answer, clerk.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: No more words. The clerk is answered.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: No more words. The clerk is answered.
They move aside. URSULA and ANTONIO move forward.
They move aside. URSULA and ANTONIO move forward.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text: I know you well enough. You are Signor Antonio.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text: I know you well enough. You are Signor Antonio.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: At a word, I am not.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text: I know you by the waggling of your head.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: To tell you true, I counterfeit him.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
You could never do him so ill-well unless you were the
very man. Here’s his dry hand up and down. You are he,
you are he.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
You could never do him so ill-well unless you were the
very man. Here’s his dry hand up and down. You are he,
you are he.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: At a word, I am not.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
Come, come, do you think I do not know you by
your excellent wit? Can virtue hide itself? Go to, mum,
you are
he. Graces will appear, and there’s an end.

They move aside. BENEDICK and BEATRICE move forward.
They move aside. BENEDICK and BEATRICE move forward.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Will you not tell me who told you so?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: No, you shall pardon me.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Nor will you not tell me who you are?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Not now.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
That I was disdainful and that I had my good wit out
of The Hundred Merry Tales! Well this was Signor
Benedick that said so.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
That I was disdainful and that I had my good wit out
of The Hundred Merry Tales! Well this was Signor
Benedick that said so.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: What’s he?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: I am sure you know him well enough.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Not I, believe me.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Did he never make you laugh?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: I pray you, what is he?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Why, he is the Prince’s jester, a very dull fool, only
his gift is in devising impossible slanders. None but
libertines delight in him, and the commendation is not
in his wit but in his villainy, for he both pleases men
and angers them, and then they laugh at him and beat
him. I am sure he is in the fleet. I would he had
boarded me.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: When I know the gentleman, I’ll tell him what you say.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Do, do. He’ll but break a comparison or two on me,
which, peradventure not marked or not laughed at,
strikes him into melancholy, and then there’s a
partridge wing saved, for the fool will eat no supper
that night.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: We must follow the leaders.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: In every good thing.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Nay, if they lead to any ill, I will leave them at the
next turning.
Dance, then exeunt all except DON JOHN, BORACHIO, and CLAUDIO

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
[to BORACHIO] Sure my brother is amorous on Hero, and
hath withdrawn her father to break with him about it.
The
ladies follow her, and but one visor remains.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
[to BORACHIO] Sure my brother is amorous on Hero, and

The
ladies follow her, and but one visor remains.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: And that is Claudio. I know him by his bearing.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: And that is Claudio. I know him by his bearing.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: [to CLAUDIO] Are not you Signor Benedick?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: You know me well. I am he.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: You know me well. I am he.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Signor, you are very near my brother in his love. He
is enamored on Hero. I pray you, dissuade him from her.
She is no equal for his birth. You may do the part of an
honest man in it.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: How know you he loves her?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: I heard him swear his affection.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: So did I too, and he swore he would marry her tonight.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: Come, let us to the banquet.
Exeunt DON JOHN and BORACHIO

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
[unmasking]
Thus answer I in the name of Benedick,
But hear these ill news with the ears of Claudio.
'Tis certain so, the Prince woos for himself.
Friendship is constant in all other things
Save in the office and affairs of love.
Therefore all hearts in love use their own tongues.
Let every eye negotiate for itself
And trust no agent, for beauty is a witch
Against whose charms faith melteth into blood.
This is an accident of hourly proof,
Which I mistrusted not. Farewell, therefore, Hero.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Count Claudio?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Yea, the same.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Come, will you go with me?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Whither?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Even to the next willow, about your own business,
county. What fashion will you wear the garland of? About
your neck like an usurer’s chain? Or under your arm
like a lieutenant’s scarf? You must wear it one way, for
the Prince hath gat your Hero.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: I wish him joy of her.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Why, that’s spoken like an honest drover; so they
sell bullocks. But did you think the Prince would have
served you thus?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:

sell bullocks. But did you think the Prince would have
served you thus?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Why, that’s spoken like an honest drover; so they
sell bullocks. But did you think the Prince would have

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: I pray you, leave me.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Ho, now you strike like the blind man. 'Twas the boy
that stole your meat, and you’ll beat the post.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Ho, now you strike like the blind man. 'Twas the boy
that stole your meat, and you’ll beat the post.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: If it will not be, I’ll leave you.
Exit

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Alas, poor hurt fowl, now will he creep into sedges.
But that my Lady Beatrice should know me, and not know
me! The Prince’s fool! Ha, it may be I go under that
title because I am merry. Yea, but so I am apt to do
myself wrong. I am not so reputed! It is the base,
though bitter, disposition of Beatrice that puts the
world into her person and so gives me out. Well, I’ll be
revenged as I may.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Alas, poor hurt fowl, now will he creep into sedges.
But that my Lady Beatrice should know me, and not know
me! The Prince’s fool! Ha, it may be I go under that
title because I am merry. Yea, but so I am apt to do
myself wrong. I am not so reputed! It is the base,
though bitter, disposition of Beatrice that puts the
world into her person and so gives me out. Well, I’ll be
revenged as I may.

Enter DON PEDRO

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Now, Signior, where’s the Count? Did you see him?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Now, Signior, where’s the Count? Did you see him?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Troth, my lord, I have played the part of Lady Fame.
I found him here as melancholy as a lodge in a warren. I
told him, and I think I told him true, that your Grace
had got the goodwill of this young lady, and I offered
him my company to a willow tree, either to make him a
garland, as being forsaken, or to bind him up a rod, as
being worthy to be whipped.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: To be whipped? What’s his fault?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: To be whipped? What’s his fault?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
The flat transgression of a schoolboy who, being
overjoyed with finding a birds' nest, shows it his
companion, and he steals it.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Wilt thou make a trust a transgression? The
transgression is in the stealer.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Wilt thou make a trust a transgression? The
transgression is in the stealer.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Yet it had not been amiss the rod had been made, and
the garland too, for the garland he might have worn
himself and the rod he might have bestowed on you, who,
as I take it, have stolen his birds' nest.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Yet it had not been amiss the rod had been made, and
the garland too, for the garland he might have worn
himself and the rod he might have bestowed on you, who,
as I take it, have stolen his birds' nest.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
I will but teach them to sing and restore them to the
owner.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
If their singing answer your saying, by my faith, you
say honestly.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
The Lady Beatrice hath a quarrel to you. The
gentleman that danced with her told her she is much
wronged by you.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
The Lady Beatrice hath a quarrel to you. The
gentleman that danced with her told her she is much
wronged by you.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
O, she misused me past the endurance of a block! An
oak but with one green leaf on it would have answered
her. My very visor began to assume life and scold with
her. She told me, not thinking I had been myself, that I
was the Prince’s jester, that I was duller than a great
thaw, huddling jest upon jest with such impossible
conveyance upon me that I stood like a man at a mark
with a whole army shooting at me. She speaks poniards,
and every word stabs. If her breath were as terrible as
her terminations, there were no living near her; she
would infect to the north star. I would not marry her,
though she were endowed with all that Adam had left him
before he transgressed. She would have made Hercules
have turned spit, yea, and have cleft his club to make
the fire, too. Come, talk not of her. You shall find her
the infernal Ate in good apparel. I would to God
some scholar would conjure her, for certainly, while she
is here, a man may live as quiet in hell as in a
sanctuary, and people sin upon purpose because they
would go thither. So indeedall disquiet, horror and
perturbation follows her.

Enter CLAUDIO, BEATRICE, HERO, and LEONATO

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Look, here she comes.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Will your grace command me any service to the
world’s end? I will go on the slightest errand now to
the Antipodes that you can devise to send me on. I will
fetch you a toothpicker now from the furthest inch of
Asia, bring you the length of Prester John’s foot, fetch
you a hair off the great Cham’s beard, do you any
embassage to the Pygmies, rather than hold three words'
conference with this harpy. You have no employment for
me?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
O God, sir, here’s a dish I love not. I cannot endure
my Lady Tongue!

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
O God, sir, here’s a dish I love not. I cannot endure
my Lady Tongue!
Exit

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
[to BEATRICE] Come, lady, come, you have lost the heart
of Signior Benedick.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
[to BEATRICE] Come, lady, come, you have lost the heart
of Signior Benedick.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Indeed, my lord, he lent it me awhile, and I gave him
use for it, a double heart for his single one. Marry,
once before he won it of me with false dice. Therefore
your Grace may well say I have lost it.

sentences.text: You have put him down, lady, you have put him down.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: You have put him down, lady, you have put him down.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
So I would not he should do me, my lord, lest I should
prove the mother of fools. I have brought Count Claudio,
whom you sent me to seek.

whom you sent me to seek.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Why, how now, Count, wherefore are you sad?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Why, how now, Count, wherefore are you sad?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Not sad, my lord.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: How then, sick?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: How then, sick?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Neither, my lord.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
The Count is neither sad, nor sick, nor merry, nor
well, but civil count, civil as an orange, and something
of that jealous complexion.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
I' faith, lady, I think your blazon to be true, though,
I’ll be sworn, if he be so, his conceit is false. —Here,
Claudio, I have wooed in thy name, and fair Hero is
won. I have broke with her father and his goodwill
obtained. Name the day of marriage, and God give thee
joy.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Count, take of me my daughter, and with her my
fortunes. His grace hath made the match, and all grace
say “Amen” to it.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Count, take of me my daughter, and with her my
fortunes. His grace hath made the match, and all grace
say “Amen” to it.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Speak, Count, ’tis your cue.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Silence is the perfectest herald of joy. I were but
little happy if I could say how much.—Lady, as you are
mine, I am yours. I give away myself for you and dote
upon the exchange.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Speak, cousin, or if you cannot, stop his mouth with a
kiss and let not him speak neither.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Speak, cousin, or if you cannot, stop his mouth with a
kiss and let not him speak neither.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: In faith, lady, you have a merry heart.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Yea, my lord. I thank it, poor fool, it keeps on the
windy side of care. My cousin tells him in his ear that
he is in her heart.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Yea, my lord. I thank it, poor fool, it keeps on the
windy side of care. My cousin tells him in his ear that
he is in her heart.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Yea, my lord. I thank it, poor fool, it keeps on the
windy side of care. My cousin tells him in his ear that
he is in her heart.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: And so she doth, cousin.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Good Lord for alliance! Thus goes everyone to the
world but I, and I am sunburnt. I may sit in a corner
and cry, “Heigh-ho for a husband!”

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:

and cry, “Heigh-ho for a husband!”

world but I, and I am sunburnt. I may sit in a corner

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Lady Beatrice, I will get you one.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I would rather have one of your father’s getting. Hath
your grace ne'er a brother like you? Your father got
excellent husbands, if a maid could come by them.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I would rather have one of your father’s getting. Hath
your grace ne'er a brother like you? Your father got
excellent husbands, if a maid could come by them.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I would rather have one of your father’s getting. Hath
your grace ne'er a brother like you? Your father got
excellent husbands, if a maid could come by them.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Will you have me, lady?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
No, my lord, unless I might have another for working
days. Your Grace is too costly to wear every day. But I
beseech your Grace pardon me. I was born to speak all
mirth and no matter.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
No, my lord, unless I might have another for working
days. Your Grace is too costly to wear every day. But I
beseech your Grace pardon me. I was born to speak all

days. Your Grace is too costly to wear every day. But I

mirth and no matter.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Your silence most offends me, and to be merry
best becomes you, for out o' question you were born in a
merry hour.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Your silence most offends me, and to be merry
best becomes you, for out o' question you were born in a
merry hour.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
No, sure, my lord, my mother cried, but then there was
a star danced, and under that was I born.—Cousins,
God give you joy!

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
No, sure, my lord, my mother cried, but then there was
a star danced, and under that was I born.—Cousins,
God give you joy!

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Niece, will you look to those things I told you of?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: I cry you mercy, uncle.—By your Grace’s pardon.
Exit

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: By my troth, a pleasant-spirited lady.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
There’s little of the melancholy element in her, my
lord. She is never sad but when she sleeps, and not ever
sad then, for I have heard my daughter say she hath
often dreamed of unhappiness and waked herself with
laughing.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: She cannot endure to hear tell of a husband.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Oh, by no means. She mocks all her wooers out of suit.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Oh, by no means. She mocks all her wooers out of suit.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: She were an excellent wife for Benedick.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
O Lord, my lord, if they were but a week married,
they would talk themselves mad.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: County Claudio, when mean you to go to church?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Tomorrow, my lord. Time goes on crutches till love
have all his rites.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Tomorrow, my lord. Time goes on crutches till love
have all his rites.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Not till Monday, my dear son, which is hence a
just sevennight, and a time too brief, too, to have all
things answer my mind.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Not till Monday, my dear son, which is hence a
just sevennight, and a time too brief, too, to have all
things answer my mind.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
[to CLAUDIO] Come, you shake the head at so long
a breathing, but I warrant thee, Claudio, the time shall
not go dully by us. I will in the interim undertake one
of Hercules' labors, which is to bring Signor Benedick
and the Lady Beatrice into a mountain of affection, th'
one with th' other. I would fain have it a match, and I
doubt not but to fashion it, if you three will but
minister such assistance as I shall give you direction.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
My lord, I am for you, though it cost me ten
nights' watchings.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: And I, my lord.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: And you too, gentle Hero?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
I will do any modest office, my lord, to help my cousin
to a good husband.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
And Benedick is not the unhopefulest husband that I
know. Thus far can I praise him: he is of a noble
strain, of approved valor, and confirmed honesty. I will
teach you how to humor your cousin that she shall fall
in love with Benedick. —And I, with your two helps, will
so practice on Benedick that, in despite of his quick
wit and his queasy stomach, he shall fall in love with
Beatrice. If we can do this, Cupid is no longer an
archer; his glory shall be ours, for we are the
only love gods. Go in with me, and I will tell you my
drift.

Enter DON JOHN and BORACHIO

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
It is so. The Count Claudio shall marry the daughter
of Leonato.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
It is so. The Count Claudio shall marry the daughter
of Leonato.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Yea, my lord, but I can cross it.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Any bar, any cross, any impediment will be med'cinable
to me. I am sick in displeasure to him, and whatsoever
comes athwart his affection ranges evenly with mine. How
canst thou cross this marriage?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Any bar, any cross, any impediment will be med'cinable
to me. I am sick in displeasure to him, and whatsoever
comes athwart his affection ranges evenly with mine. How
canst thou cross this marriage?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Any bar, any cross, any impediment will be med'cinable
to me. I am sick in displeasure to him, and whatsoever
comes athwart his affection ranges evenly with mine. How
canst thou cross this marriage?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Not honestly, my lord, but so covertly that no
dishonesty shall appear in me.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: Show me briefly how.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
I think I told your lordship a year since how much I am
in the favor of Margaret, the waiting gentlewoman to
Hero.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: I remember.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
I can, at any unseasonable instant of the night,
appoint her to look out at her lady’s chamber window.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: What life is in that to be the death of this marriage?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
The poison of that lies in you to temper. Go you to
the Prince your brother. Spare not to tell him that he
hath wronged his honor in marrying the renowned
Claudio, whose estimation do you mightily hold up, to
a contaminated stale, such a one as Hero.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
The poison of that lies in you to temper. Go you to
the Prince your brother. Spare not to tell him that he
hath wronged his honor in marrying the renowned
Claudio, whose estimation do you mightily hold up, to
a contaminated stale, such a one as Hero.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
The poison of that lies in you to temper. Go you to
the Prince your brother. Spare not to tell him that he
hath wronged his honor in marrying the renowned
Claudio, whose estimation do you mightily hold up, to
a contaminated stale, such a one as Hero.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: What proof shall I make of that?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Proof enough to misuse the Prince, to vex Claudio, to
undo Hero, and kill Leonato. Look you for any other
issue?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Proof enough to misuse the Prince, to vex Claudio, to
undo Hero, and kill Leonato. Look you for any other
issue?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: Only to despite them, I will endeavor anything.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Go then, find me a meet hour to draw Don Pedro and
the Count Claudio alone. Tell them that you know that
Hero loves me. Intend a kind of zeal both to the Prince
and Claudio, as in love of your brother’s honor, who
hath made this match, and his friend’s reputation, who
is thus like to be cozened with the semblance of a maid,
that you have discovered thus. They will scarcely
believe this without trial. Offer them instances, which
shall bear no less likelihood than to see me at her
chamber window, hear me call Margaret “Hero,” hear
Margaret term me “Claudio,” and bring them to see this
the very night before the intended wedding, for in the
meantime I will so fashion the matter that Hero shall be
absent, and there shall appear such seeming truth of
Hero’s disloyalty that jealousy shall be called
assurance and all the preparation overthrown.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Grow this to what adverse issue it can, I will put it
in practice. Be cunning in the working this, and thy fee
is a thousand ducats.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Grow this to what adverse issue it can, I will put it
in practice. Be cunning in the working this, and thy fee
is a thousand ducats.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Be you constant in the accusation, and my cunning
shall not
shame me.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: I will presently go learn their day of marriage.
Exeunt
Enter BENEDICK

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Boy!
Enter BOY

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: Signior?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
In my chamber window lies a book. Bring it hither to me
in the orchard.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
In my chamber window lies a book. Bring it hither to me
in the orchard.

sentences.speaker: BOY
sentences.text: I am here already, sir.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I know that, but I would have thee hence and here
again.
Exit BOY

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I do much wonder that one man, seeing how much
another man is a fool when he dedicates his behaviors to
love, will, after he hath laughed at such shallow
follies in others, become the argument of his own scorn
by falling in love— and such a man is Claudio. I have
known when there was no music with him but the drum and
the fife, and now had he rather hear the tabor and the
pipe. I have known when he would have walked ten mile
afoot to see a good armor, and now will he lie ten
nights awake carving the fashion of a new doublet. He
was wont to speak plain and to the purpose, like an
honest man and a soldier, and now is he
turned orthography; his words are a very fantastical
banquet, just so many strange dishes. May I be so
converted and see with these eyes? I cannot tell; I
think not. I will not be sworn but love may transform me
to an oyster, but I’ll take my oath on it, till he have
made an oyster of me, he shall never make me such a
fool. One woman is fair, yet I am well; another is wise,
yet I am well; another virtuous, yet I am well; but
till all graces be in one woman, one woman shall not
come in my grace. Rich she shall be, that’s certain;
wise, or I’ll none; virtuous, or I’ll never cheapen her;
fair, or I’ll ever look on her; mild, or come not
near me; noble, or not I for an angel; of good

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I do much wonder that one man, seeing how much
another man is a fool when he dedicates his behaviors to
love, will, after he hath laughed at such shallow
follies in others, become the argument of his own scorn
by falling in love— and such a man is Claudio. I have
known when there was no music with him but the drum and

pipe. I have known when he would have walked ten mile

nights awake carving the fashion of a new doublet. He
was wont to speak plain and to the purpose, like an
honest man and a soldier, and now is he
turned orthography; his words are a very fantastical
banquet, just so many strange dishes. May I be so
converted and see with these eyes? I cannot tell; I
think not. I will not be sworn but love may transform me
to an oyster, but I’ll take my oath on it, till he have
made an oyster of me, he shall never make me such a
fool. One woman is fair, yet I am well; another is wise,
yet I am well; another virtuous, yet I am well; but
till all graces be in one woman, one woman shall not
come in my grace. Rich she shall be, that’s certain;

fair, or I’ll ever look on her; mild, or come not
near me; noble, or not I for an angel; of good
discourse, an excellent musician, and her hair shall be
of what color it please God. Ha! The Prince and Monsieur
Love! I will hide me in the arbor.

come in my grace. Rich she shall be, that’s certain;
wise, or I’ll none; virtuous, or I’ll never cheapen her;
fair, or I’ll ever look on her; mild, or come not
near me; noble, or not I for an angel; of good
discourse, an excellent musician, and her hair shall be
of what color it please God. Ha! The Prince and Monsieur
Love! I will hide me in the arbor.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I do much wonder that one man, seeing how much
another man is a fool when he dedicates his behaviors to
love, will, after he hath laughed at such shallow
follies in others, become the argument of his own scorn
by falling in love— and such a man is Claudio. I have
known when there was no music with him but the drum and
the fife, and now had he rather hear the tabor and the
pipe. I have known when he would have walked ten mile
afoot to see a good armor, and now will he lie ten
nights awake carving the fashion of a new doublet. He
was wont to speak plain and to the purpose, like an
honest man and a soldier, and now is he
turned orthography; his words are a very fantastical
banquet, just so many strange dishes. May I be so
converted and see with these eyes? I cannot tell; I
think not. I will not be sworn but love may transform me
to an oyster, but I’ll take my oath on it, till he have
made an oyster of me, he shall never make me such a
fool. One woman is fair, yet I am well; another is wise,
yet I am well; another virtuous, yet I am well; but
till all graces be in one woman, one woman shall not
come in my grace. Rich she shall be, that’s certain;
wise, or I’ll none; virtuous, or I’ll never cheapen her;
fair, or I’ll ever look on her; mild, or come not
near me; noble, or not I for an angel; of good
discourse, an excellent musician, and her hair shall be
of what color it please God. Ha! The Prince and Monsieur
Love! I will hide me in the arbor.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Come, shall we hear this music?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Yea, my good lord. How still the evening is,
As hushed on purpose to grace harmony!

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Yea, my good lord. How still the evening is,
As hushed on purpose to grace harmony!

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
[aside to CLAUDIO] See you where Benedick hath hid
himself?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
[aside to DON PEDRO]
O, very well, my lord. The music ended,
We’ll fit the kid-fox with a pennyworth.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
[aside to DON PEDRO]

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Come, Balthasar, we’ll hear that song again.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text:
O, good my lord, tax not so bad a voice
To slander music anymore than once.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
It is the witness still of excellency
To put a strange face on his own perfection.
I pray thee, sing, and let me woo no more.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
It is the witness still of excellency
To put a strange face on his own perfection.
I pray thee, sing, and let me woo no more.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text:
Because you talk of wooing, I will sing,
Since many a wooer doth commence his suit
To her he thinks not worthy, yet he woos,
Yet will he swear he loves.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Nay, pray thee, come,
Or, if thou wilt hold longer argument,
Do it in notes.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Nay, pray thee, come,

Do it in notes.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR

Note this before my notes:
There’s not a note of mine that’s worth the noting.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Why, these are very crotchets that he speaks!
Note notes, forsooth, and nothing.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
[aside] Now, divine air! Now is his soul ravished. Is
it not strange that sheeps' guts should hale souls out
of men’s bodies? Well, a horn for my money, when all’s
done.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text:
[singing]
Sigh no more, ladies, sigh no more,
Men were deceivers ever,
One foot in sea and one on shore,
To one thing constant never.
Then sigh not so, but let them go,
And be you blithe and bonny,
Converting all your sounds of woe
Into Hey, nonny nonny.
Sing no more ditties, sing no mo
Of dumps so dull and heavy.
The fraud of men was ever so,
Since summer first was leavy.
Then sigh not so, but let them go
And be you blithe and bonny,
Converting all your sounds of woe
Into Hey, nonny nonny.

sentences.speaker: BALTHASAR
sentences.text: And an ill singer, my lord.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Ha, no, no, faith, thou sing’st well enough for a
shift.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO

[aside] An he had been a dog that should have howled
thus, they would have hanged him. And I pray God his bad
voice
bode no mischief. I had as lief have heard the night
raven, come what plague could have come after it.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Come hither, Leonato. What was it you told me of
today, that your niece Beatrice was in love with Signor
Benedick?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Come hither, Leonato. What was it you told me of
today, that your niece Beatrice was in love with Signor
Benedick?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Oh, ay. [aside to DON PEDRO] Stalk on, stalk on; the
fowl sits.—I did never think that lady would have loved
any man.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
No, nor I neither, but most wonderful that she should
so dote on Signor Benedick, whom she hath in all
outward behaviors seemed ever to abhor.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
No, nor I neither, but most wonderful that she should
so dote on Signor Benedick, whom she hath in all
outward behaviors seemed ever to abhor.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: [aside] Is ’t possible? Sits the wind in that corner?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: [aside] Is ’t possible? Sits the wind in that corner?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
By my troth, my lord, I cannot tell what to think of
it, but that she loves him with an enraged affection, it
is past the infinite of thought.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
By my troth, my lord, I cannot tell what to think of
it, but that she loves him with an enraged affection, it
is past the infinite of thought.

sentences.text: May be she doth but counterfeit.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Faith, like enough.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
O God! Counterfeit? There was never counterfeit
of passion came so near the life of passion as she
discovers it.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
O God! Counterfeit? There was never counterfeit
of passion came so near the life of passion as she
discovers it.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
[aside to LEONATO] Bait the hook well; this fish will
bite.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
What effects, my lord? She will sit you—you heard
my daughter tell you how.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
What effects, my lord? She will sit you—you heard
my daughter tell you how.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: She did indeed.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
How, how I pray you? You amaze me. I would have
thought her spirit had been invincible against all
assaults of affection.

sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
[Aside] I should think this a gull but that the
white-bearded fellow speaks it. Knavery cannot, sure,
hide himself in such reverence.

[aside to DON PEDRO] He hath ta'en th' infection. Hold
it up.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: No, and swears she never will. That’s her torment.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: No, and swears she never will. That’s her torment.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
'Tis true indeed, so your daughter says. “Shall I,”
says she, “that have so oft encountered him with scorn,
write to him that I love him?”

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO

This says she now when she is beginning to write to

Now you talk of a sheet of paper, I remember a pretty
jest your daughter told.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Oh, when she had writ it and was reading it over, she
found “Benedick” and “Beatrice” between the sheet?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: That.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
O, she tore the letter into a thousand halfpence,
railed at herself that she should be so immodest to
write to one that she knew would flout her. “I measure
him,” says she, “by my own spirit, for I should flout
him if he writ to me, yea, though I love him, I should.”

sentences.text:
Then down upon her knees she falls, weeps, sobs, beats
her heart, tears her hair, prays, curses: “O sweet
Benedick! God give me patience!”

sentences.text:
She doth indeed, my daughter says so, and the ecstasy
hath so much overborne her that my daughter is sometime
afeared she will do a desperate outrage to herself. It
is very true.

It were good that Benedick knew of it by some other, if
she will not discover it.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
To what end? He would make but a sport of it and
torment the poor lady worse.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
To what end? He would make but a sport of it and
torment the poor lady worse.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
An he should, it were an alms to hang him. She’s
an excellent sweet lady, and, out of all suspicion, she
is virtuous.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
An he should, it were an alms to hang him. She’s
an excellent sweet lady, and, out of all suspicion, she
is virtuous.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: And she is exceeding wise.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: In every thing but in loving Benedick.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Oh, my lord, wisdom and blood combating in so tender
a body, we have ten proofs to one that blood hath the
victory. I am sorry for her, as I have just cause, being
her uncle and her guardian.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
I would she had bestowed this dotage on me. I would
have daffed all other respects and made her half myself.
I pray you tell Benedick of it and hear what he will
say.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Were it good, think you?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Hero thinks surely she will die, for she says she will
die if he love her not, and she will die ere she make
her love known, and she will die if he woo her rather
than she will bate one breath of her accustomed
crossness.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Hero thinks surely she will die, for she says she will

her love known, and she will die if he woo her rather
than she will bate one breath of her accustomed
crossness.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
She doth well. If she should make tender of her love,
’tis very possible he’ll scorn it, for the man, as you
know all, hath a contemptible spirit.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: He is a very proper man.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: He hath indeed a good outward happiness.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Before God, and in my mind, very wise.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: He doth indeed show some sparks that are like wit.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: And I take him to be valiant.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
As Hector, I assure you, and in the managing of
quarrels you may say he is wise, for either he avoids
them with great discretion or undertakes them with a
most Christian-like fear.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
As Hector, I assure you, and in the managing of
quarrels you may say he is wise, for either he avoids
them with great discretion or undertakes them with a
most Christian-like fear.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
If he do fear God, he must necessarily keep peace. If
he break the peace, he ought to enter into a quarrel
with fear and trembling.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
If he do fear God, he must necessarily keep peace. If
he break the peace, he ought to enter into a quarrel
with fear and trembling.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
And so will he do, for the man doth fear God,
howsoever it seems not in him by some large jests he
will make. Well, I am sorry for your niece. Shall we go
seek Benedick and tell him of her love?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:

howsoever it seems not in him by some large jests he

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Never tell him, my lord, let her wear it out with
good counsel.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Never tell him, my lord, let her wear it out with
good counsel.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Nay, that’s impossible. She may wear her heart out
first.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Nay, that’s impossible. She may wear her heart out
first.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, we will hear further of it by your daughter. Let
it cool the while. I love Benedick well, and I could
wish he would modestly examine himself to see how much
he is unworthy so good a lady.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, we will hear further of it by your daughter. Let
it cool the while. I love Benedick well, and I could
wish he would modestly examine himself to see how much
he is unworthy so good a lady.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Well, we will hear further of it by your daughter. Let
it cool the while. I love Benedick well, and I could
wish he would modestly examine himself to see how much
he is unworthy so good a lady.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: My lord, will you walk? Dinner is ready.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: My lord, will you walk? Dinner is ready.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
[aside to DON PEDRO and LEONATO] If he do not dote on
her upon this, I will never trust my expectation.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
[aside to LEONATO] Let there be the same net spread for
her, and that must your daughter and her gentlewomen
carry. The sport will be when they hold one an opinion
of another’s dotage, and no such matter. That’s the
scene that I would see, which will be merely a dumb
show. Let us send her to call him in to dinner.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
[aside to LEONATO] Let there be the same net spread for
her, and that must your daughter and her gentlewomen
carry. The sport will be when they hold one an opinion

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
[coming forward] This can be no trick. The conference
was sadly borne; they have the truth of this from Hero;
they seem to pity the lady. It seems her affections have
their full bent. Love me? Why, it must be requited! I
hear how I am censured. They sy I will bear myself
proudly if I perceive the love come from her. They say,
too, that she will rather die than give any sign of
affection. I did never think to marry. I must not seem
proud. Happy are they that hear their detractions and
can put them to mending. They say the lady is fair; ’tis
a truth, I can bear them witness. And virtuous; ’tis
so, I cannot reprove it. And wise, but for loving me; by
my troth, it is no addition to her wit, nor no great
argument of her folly, for I will be horribly in
love with her! I may chance have some odd quirks and
remnants of wit broken on me because I have railed so
long against marriage, but doth not the appetite alter?
A man loves the meat in his youth that he cannot endure
in his age. Shall quips and sentences and these paper
bullets of the brain awe a man from the career of his
humor? No! The world must be peopled. When I said I
would die a bachelor, I did not think I should live till
I were married. Here comes Beatrice. By this day, she’s
a fair lady. I do spy some marks of love in her.

remnants of wit broken on me because I have railed so
long against marriage, but doth not the appetite alter?
A man loves the meat in his youth that he cannot endure
in his age. Shall quips and sentences and these paper
bullets of the brain awe a man from the career of his
humor? No! The world must be peopled. When I said I
would die a bachelor, I did not think I should live till
I were married. Here comes Beatrice. By this day, she’s
a fair lady. I do spy some marks of love in her.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
[coming forward] This can be no trick. The conference
was sadly borne; they have the truth of this from Hero;
they seem to pity the lady. It seems her affections have
their full bent. Love me? Why, it must be requited! I
hear how I am censured. They sy I will bear myself
proudly if I perceive the love come from her. They say,
too, that she will rather die than give any sign of
affection. I did never think to marry. I must not seem
proud. Happy are they that hear their detractions and
can put them to mending. They say the lady is fair; ’tis
a truth, I can bear them witness. And virtuous; ’tis
so, I cannot reprove it. And wise, but for loving me; by
my troth, it is no addition to her wit, nor no great
argument of her folly, for I will be horribly in
love with her! I may chance have some odd quirks and

Enter BEATRICE

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Against my will, I am sent to bid you come in to
dinner.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Fair Beatrice, I thank you for your pains.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I took no more pains for those thanks than you take
pains to thank me. If it had been painful, I would not
have come.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I took no more pains for those thanks than you take
pains to thank me. If it had been painful, I would not
have come.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: You take pleasure then in the message?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Yea, just so much as you may take upon a knife’s point
and choke a daw withal. You have no stomach, Signior.
Fare you well.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Yea, just so much as you may take upon a knife’s point
and choke a daw withal. You have no stomach, Signior.
Fare you well.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:

and choke a daw withal. You have no stomach, Signior.

Exit

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Ha! “Against my will I am sent to bid you come in
to dinner.” There’s a double meaning in that. “I took no
more pains for those thanks than you took pains to
thank me.” That’s as much as to say, “Any pains that I
take for you is as easy as thanks.” If I do not take
pity of her, I am a villain. If I do not love her, I am
a Jew. I will go get her picture.

Enter HERO, MARGARET, and URSULA

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Good Margaret, run thee to the parlor.
There shalt thou find my cousin Beatrice
Proposing with the Prince and Claudio.
Whisper her ear and tell her I and Ursula
Walk in the orchard, and our whole discourse
Is all of her. Say that thou overheardst us,
And bid her steal into the pleachèd bower
Where honeysuckles ripened by the sun
Forbid the sun to enter, like favorites
Made proud by princes, that advance their pride
Against that power that bred it. There will she hide
her
To listen our propose. This is thy office.
Bear thee well in it and leave us alone.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: I’ll make her come, I warrant you, presently.
Exit

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Now, Ursula, when Beatrice doth come,
As we do trace this alley up and down,
Our talk must only be of Benedick.
When I do name him, let it be thy part
To praise him more than ever man did merit.
My talk to thee must be how Benedick
Is sick in love with Beatrice. Of this matter
Is little Cupid’s crafty arrow made,
That only wounds by hearsay.

Enter BEATRICE, behind

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Now begin,
For look where Beatrice like a lapwing runs
Close by the ground, to hear our conference.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
[aside to HERO]
The pleasant’st angling is to see the fish
Cut with her golden oars the silver stream
And greedily devour the treacherous bait.
So angle we for Beatrice, who even now
Is couchèd in the woodbine coverture.
Fear you not my part of the dialogue.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
[aside to HERO]

Is couchèd in the woodbine coverture.
Fear you not my part of the dialogue.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
[aside to URSULA ]
Then go we near her, that her ear lose nothing
Of the false sweet bait that we lay for it.—
[approaching the bower]
No, truly, Ursula, she is too disdainful.
I know her spirits are as coy and wild
As haggards of the rock.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
[aside to URSULA ]

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
But are you sure
That Benedick loves Beatrice so entirely?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: So says the Prince and my new-trothèd lord.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text: And did they bid you tell her of it, madam?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
They did entreat me to acquaint her of it,
But I persuaded them, if they loved Benedick,
To wish him wrestle with affection
And never to let Beatrice know of it.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
They did entreat me to acquaint her of it,
But I persuaded them, if they loved Benedick,
To wish him wrestle with affection
And never to let Beatrice know of it.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
Why did you so? Doth not the gentleman
Deserve as full as fortunate a bed
As ever Beatrice shall couch upon?

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
Why did you so? Doth not the gentleman
Deserve as full as fortunate a bed
As ever Beatrice shall couch upon?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
O god of love! I know he doth deserve
As much as may be yielded to a man,
But Nature never framed a woman’s heart
Of prouder stuff than that of Beatrice.
Disdain and scorn ride sparkling in her eyes,
Misprizing what they look on, and her wit
Values itself so highly that to her
All matter else seems weak. She cannot love
Nor take no shape nor project of affection
She is so self-endeared.

Sure, I think so,
And therefore certainly it were not good
She knew his love, lest she make sport at it.

URSULA
sentences.text: Sure, sure, such carping is not commendable.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
No, not to be so odd and from all fashions
As Beatrice is, cannot be commendable.
But who dare tell her so? If I should speak,
She would mock me into air. O, she would laugh me
Out of myself, press me to death with wit.
Therefore let Benedick, like covered fire,
Consume away in sighs, waste inwardly.
It were a better death than die with mocks,
Which is as bad as die with tickling.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text: Yet tell her of it. Hear what she will say.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
No, rather I will go to Benedick
And counsel him to fight against his passion;
And truly I’ll devise some honest slanders
To stain my cousin with. One doth not know
How much an ill word may empoison liking.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
O, do not do your cousin such a wrong!
She cannot be so much without true judgment,
Having so swift and excellent a wit
As she is prized to have, as to refuse
So rare a gentleman as Signior Benedick.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:

She cannot be so much without true judgment,
Having so swift and excellent a wit
As she is prized to have, as to refuse
So rare a gentleman as Signior Benedick.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
He is the only man of Italy,
Always excepted my dear Claudio.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
I pray you, be not angry with me, madam,
Speaking my fancy. Signor Benedick,
For shape, for bearing, argument and valor,
Goes foremost in report through Italy.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Indeed, he hath an excellent good name.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
His excellence did earn it, ere he had it.
When are you married, madam?

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
His excellence did earn it, ere he had it.
When are you married, madam?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Why, every day, tomorrow. Come, go in.
I’ll show thee some attires, and have thy counsel
Which is the best to furnish me tomorrow.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Why, every day, tomorrow. Come, go in.
I’ll show thee some attires, and have thy counsel
Which is the best to furnish me tomorrow.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Why, every day, tomorrow. Come, go in.
I’ll show thee some attires, and have thy counsel
Which is the best to furnish me tomorrow.
They move aside from the bower

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
[aside to HERO]
She’s limed, I warrant you. We have caught her, madam.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
[aside to HERO]
She’s limed, I warrant you. We have caught her, madam.

sentences.speaker: HERO
HERO

sentences.text: Exeunt HERO and URSULA

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
[coming forward]
What fire is in mine ears? Can this be true?
Stand I condemned for pride and scorn so much?
Contempt, farewell, and maiden pride, adieu!
No glory lives behind the back of such.
And Benedick, love on; I will requite thee,
Taming my wild heart to thy loving hand.
If thou dost love, my kindness shall incite thee
To bind our loves up in a holy band.
For others say thou dost deserve, and I
Believe it better than reportingly.

Enter DON PEDRO, CLAUDIO, BENEDICK, and LEONATO

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
I do but stay till your marriage be consummate, and
then go I toward Aragon.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
I’ll bring you thither, my lord, if you’ll vouchsafe
me.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Nay, that would be as great a soil in the new gloss of
your marriage as to show a child his new coat and forbid
him to wear it. I will only be bold with Benedick for
his company, for from the crown of his head to the sole
of his foot he is all mirth. He hath twice or thrice cut
Cupid’s bow-string, and the little hangman dare not
shoot at him. He hath a heart as sound as a bell, and
his tongue is the clapper, for what his heart thinks,
his tongue speaks.
