sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Gallants, I am not as I have been.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: So say I. Methinks you are sadder.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: So say I. Methinks you are sadder.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: I hope he be in love.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Hang him, truant! There’s no true drop of blood in him
to be truly touched with love. If he be sad, he wants
money.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Hang him, truant! There’s no true drop of blood in him
to be truly touched with love. If he be sad, he wants
money.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Hang him, truant! There’s no true drop of blood in him
to be truly touched with love. If he be sad, he wants
money.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: I have the toothache.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Draw it.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Hang it!

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: You must hang it first, and draw it afterwards.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: What, sigh for the toothache?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Where is but a humor or a worm.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Well, everyone can master a grief but he that has it.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Yet say I, he is in love.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
There is no appearance of fancy in him, unless it be a
fancy that he hath to strange disguises, as to be a
Dutchman today, a Frenchman tomorrow, or in the shape of
two countries at once, as a German from the waist
downward, all slops, and a Spaniard from the hip upward,
no doublet. Unless he have a fancy to this foolery, as
it appears he hath, he is no fool for fancy, as you
would have it appear he is.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
If he be not in love with some woman, there is no
believing old signs. He brushes his hat o' mornings.
What should that bode?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
If he be not in love with some woman, there is no
believing old signs. He brushes his hat o' mornings.
What should that bode?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
If he be not in love with some woman, there is no
believing old signs. He brushes his hat o' mornings.
What should that bode?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Hath any man seen him at the barber’s?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
No, but the barber’s man hath been seen with him, and
the old ornament of his cheek hath already stuffed
tennis balls.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
No, but the barber’s man hath been seen with him, and
the old ornament of his cheek hath already stuffed
tennis balls.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Indeed, he looks younger than he did, by the loss of a
beard.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Nay, he rubs himself with civet. Can you smell him out
by that?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Nay, he rubs himself with civet. Can you smell him out
by that?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: That’s as much as to say, the sweet youth’s in love.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: The greatest note of it is his melancholy.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: And when was he wont to wash his face?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Yea, or to paint himself? For the which I hear what
they say of him.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Yea, or to paint himself? For the which I hear what
they say of him.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Nay, but his jesting spirit, which is now crept into a
lute string and now governed by stops—

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Nay, but his jesting spirit, which is now crept into a
lute string and now governed by stops—

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Indeed, that tells a heavy tale for him. Conclude,
conclude, he is in love.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Indeed, that tells a heavy tale for him. Conclude,
conclude, he is in love.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Nay, but I know who loves him.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
That would I know too. I warrant, one that knows him
not.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
That would I know too. I warrant, one that knows him
not.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Yes, and his ill conditions, and, in despite of all,
dies for him.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Yes, and his ill conditions, and, in despite of all,
dies for him.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: She shall be buried with her face upwards.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Yet is this no charm for the toothache.—Old Signior,
walk aside with me. I have studied eight or nine wise
words to speak to you, which these hobbyhorses must not
hear.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Yet is this no charm for the toothache.—Old Signior,
walk aside with me. I have studied eight or nine wise
words to speak to you, which these hobbyhorses must not
hear.
Exeunt BENEDICK and LEONATO

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: For my life, to break with him about Beatrice!

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
'Tis even so. Hero and Margaret have by this played
their parts with Beatrice, and then the two bears will
not bite one another when they meet.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
'Tis even so. Hero and Margaret have by this played
their parts with Beatrice, and then the two bears will
not bite one another when they meet.

Enter DON JOHN

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: My lord and brother, God save you.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Good e'en, brother.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: If your leisure served, I would speak with you.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: In private?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
If it please you. Yet Count Claudio may hear, for what
I would speak of concerns him.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
If it please you. Yet Count Claudio may hear, for what
I would speak of concerns him.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: What’s the matter?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
[to CLAUDIO] Means your lordship to be married
tomorrow?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: You know he does.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: I know not that, when he knows what I know.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: If there be any impediment, I pray you discover it.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
You may think I love you not. Let that appear
hereafter, and aim better at me by that I now will
manifest. For my brother, I think he holds you well, and
in dearness of heart hath holp to effect your ensuing
marriage—surely suit ill spent and labor ill bestowed.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
You may think I love you not. Let that appear
hereafter, and aim better at me by that I now will
manifest. For my brother, I think he holds you well, and
in dearness of heart hath holp to effect your ensuing
marriage—surely suit ill spent and labor ill bestowed.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
You may think I love you not. Let that appear
hereafter, and aim better at me by that I now will
manifest. For my brother, I think he holds you well, and
in dearness of heart hath holp to effect your ensuing
marriage—surely suit ill spent and labor ill bestowed.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Why, what’s the matter?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
I came hither to tell you; and, circumstances
shortened, for she has been too long a-talking of, the
lady is disloyal.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Who, Hero?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text: Even she: Leonato’s Hero, your Hero, every man’s Hero.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Disloyal?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
The word is too good to paint out her wickedness. I
could say she were worse. Think you of a worse title,
and I will fit her to it. Wonder not till further
warrant. Go but with me tonight, you shall see her
chamber window entered, even the night before her
wedding day. If you love her then, tomorrow wed her. But
it would better fit your honor to
change your mind.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: [to DON PEDRO] May this be so?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: I will not think it.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
If you dare not trust that you see, confess not that
you know. If you will follow me, I will show you enough,
and when you have seen more and heard more, proceed
accordingly.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
If you dare not trust that you see, confess not that
you know. If you will follow me, I will show you enough,
and when you have seen more and heard more, proceed
accordingly.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
If you dare not trust that you see, confess not that
you know. If you will follow me, I will show you enough,
and when you have seen more and heard more, proceed
accordingly.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
If I see anything tonight why I should not marry
her, tomorrow in the congregation, where I should wed,
there will I shame her.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
And as I wooed for thee to obtain her, I will join with
thee to disgrace her.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
I will disparage her no farther till you are my
witnesses. Bear it coldly but till midnight and let the
issue show itself.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
I will disparage her no farther till you are my
witnesses. Bear it coldly but till midnight and let the
issue show itself.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: O day untowardly turned!

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: O mischief strangely thwarting!

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
O plague right well prevented! So will you say when
you have seen the sequel.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
O plague right well prevented! So will you say when
you have seen the sequel.
Exeunt
Enter DOGBERRY and VERGES with the Watch

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Are you good men and true?

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text:
Yea, or else it were pity but they should suffer
salvation, body and soul.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Nay, that were a punishment too good for them, if
they should have any allegiance in them, being chosen
for the Prince’s watch.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Well, give them their charge, neighbor Dogberry.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
First, who think you the most desartless man to be
constable?

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Hugh Oatcake, sir, or George Seacole, for they can
write and read.

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Hugh Oatcake, sir, or George Seacole, for they can
write and read.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Come hither, neighbor Seacole. God hath blessed you
with a good name. To be a well-favored man is the gift
of fortune, but to write and read comes by nature.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Come hither, neighbor Seacole. God hath blessed you
with a good name. To be a well-favored man is the gift
of fortune, but to write and read comes by nature.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Come hither, neighbor Seacole. God hath blessed you
with a good name. To be a well-favored man is the gift
of fortune, but to write and read comes by nature.

sentences.speaker: SEACOLE
sentences.text: Both which, Master Constable—

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
You have. I knew it would be your answer. Well, for
your favor, sir, why, give God thanks, and make no boast
of it, and for your writing and reading, let that
appear when there is no need of such vanity. You are
thought here to be the most senseless and fit man for
the constable of the watch; therefore bear you the
lantern. This is your charge: you shall comprehend all
vagrom men; you are to bid any man stand, in the
Prince's name.

of it, and for your writing and reading, let that
appear when there is no need of such vanity. You are
thought here to be the most senseless and fit man for
the constable of the watch; therefore bear you the
lantern. This is your charge: you shall comprehend all
vagrom men; you are to bid any man stand, in the
Prince's name.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
You have. I knew it would be your answer. Well, for
your favor, sir, why, give God thanks, and make no boast
of it, and for your writing and reading, let that
appear when there is no need of such vanity. You are
thought here to be the most senseless and fit man for
the constable of the watch; therefore bear you the
lantern. This is your charge: you shall comprehend all
vagrom men; you are to bid any man stand, in the
Prince's name.

sentences.speaker: SECOND WATCHMAN
sentences.text: How if he will not stand?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why, then, take no note of him, but let him go
and presently call the rest of the watch together and
thank God you are rid of a knave.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why, then, take no note of him, but let him go
and presently call the rest of the watch together and
thank God you are rid of a knave.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text:
If he will not stand when he is bidden, he is none of
the Prince’s subjects.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
True, and they are to meddle with none but the
Prince’s subjects.—You shall also make no noise in the
streets; for, for the watch to babble and to talk is
most tolerable and not to be endured.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
True, and they are to meddle with none but the
Prince’s subjects.—You shall also make no noise in the
streets; for, for the watch to babble and to talk is
most tolerable and not to be endured.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
We will rather sleep than talk. We know what belongs to
a watch.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
We will rather sleep than talk. We know what belongs to
a watch.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why, you speak like an ancient and most quiet
watchman, for I cannot see how sleeping should offend.
Only have a care that your bills be not stolen. Well, you
are to call at all the alehouses and bid those that are
drunk get them to bed.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why, you speak like an ancient and most quiet
watchman, for I cannot see how sleeping should offend.
Only have a care that your bills be not stolen. Well, you
are to call at all the alehouses and bid those that are
drunk get them to bed.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why, you speak like an ancient and most quiet
watchman, for I cannot see how sleeping should offend.
Only have a care that your bills be not stolen. Well, you
are to call at all the alehouses and bid those that are
drunk get them to bed.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text: How if they will not?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why, then, let them alone till they are sober. If they
make you not then the better answer, you may say they
are not the men you took them for.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why, then, let them alone till they are sober. If they
make you not then the better answer, you may say they
are not the men you took them for.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text: Well, sir.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
If you meet a thief, you may suspect him, by virtue of
your office, to be no true man, and for such kind of
men, the less you meddle or make with them, why the more
is for your honesty.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
If you meet a thief, you may suspect him, by virtue of
your office, to be no true man, and for such kind of
men, the less you meddle or make with them, why the more
is for your honesty.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
If we know him to be a thief, shall we not lay hands
on him?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Truly, by your office you may, but I think they that
touch pitch will be defiled. The most peaceable way for
you, if you do take a thief, is to let him show himself
what he is and steal out of your company.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Truly, by your office you may, but I think they that
touch pitch will be defiled. The most peaceable way for
you, if you do take a thief, is to let him show himself
what he is and steal out of your company.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: You have been always called a merciful man, partner.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Truly, I would not hang a dog by my will, much more a
man who hath any honesty in him.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text:
[to the Watch] If you hear a child cry in the night,
you must call to the nurse and bid her still it.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text: How if the nurse be asleep and will not hear us?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why then, depart in peace and let the child wake her
with crying, for the ewe that will not hear her lamb
when it baas will never answer a calf when he bleats.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Why then, depart in peace and let the child wake her
with crying, for the ewe that will not hear her lamb
when it baas will never answer a calf when he bleats.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: 'Tis very true.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
This is the end of the charge. You, constable, are to
present the Prince’s own person. If you meet the Prince
in the night, you may stay him.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
This is the end of the charge. You, constable, are to
present the Prince’s own person. If you meet the Prince
in the night, you may stay him.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
This is the end of the charge. You, constable, are to
present the Prince’s own person. If you meet the Prince
in the night, you may stay him.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Nay, by 'r Lady, that I think he cannot.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Five shillings to one on ’t, with any man that knows
the statutes, he may stay him —marry, not without the
Prince be willing, for indeed the watch ought to offend
no man, and it is an offense to stay a man against his
will.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Five shillings to one on ’t, with any man that knows
the statutes, he may stay him —marry, not without the
Prince be willing, for indeed the watch ought to offend
no man, and it is an offense to stay a man against his
will.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: By 'r lady, I think it be so.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Ha, ha, ha! Well, masters, good night. An there be any
matter of weight chances, call up me. Keep your
fellows' counsels and your own; and good night.—Come,
neighbor.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Ha, ha, ha! Well, masters, good night. An there be any
matter of weight chances, call up me. Keep your
fellows' counsels and your own; and good night.—Come,
neighbor.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:

matter of weight chances, call up me. Keep your
fellows' counsels and your own; and good night.—Come,
neighbor.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Ha, ha, ha! Well, masters, good night. An there be any
matter of weight chances, call up me. Keep your
fellows' counsels and your own; and good night.—Come,
neighbor.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Ha, ha, ha! Well, masters, good night. An there be any
matter of weight chances, call up me. Keep your
fellows' counsels and your own; and good night.—Come,
neighbor.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Well, masters, we hear our charge. Let us go sit here
upon the church bench till two, and then all to bed.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Well, masters, we hear our charge. Let us go sit here
upon the church bench till two, and then all to bed.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
One word more, honest neighbors. I pray you watch
about Signior Leonato’s door, for the wedding being
there tomorrow, there is a great coil tonight. Adieu, be
vigitant, I beseech you.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
One word more, honest neighbors. I pray you watch
about Signior Leonato’s door, for the wedding being
there tomorrow, there is a great coil tonight. Adieu, be
vigitant, I beseech you.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
One word more, honest neighbors. I pray you watch
about Signior Leonato’s door, for the wedding being
there tomorrow, there is a great coil tonight. Adieu, be
vigitant, I beseech you.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
One word more, honest neighbors. I pray you watch
about Signior Leonato’s door, for the wedding being
there tomorrow, there is a great coil tonight. Adieu, be
vigitant, I beseech you.
Exeunt DOGBERRY and VERGES
Enter BORACHIO and CONRAD

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: What Conrad!

sentences.speaker: SEACOALE
sentences.text: [aside] Peace! Stir not.

sentences.speaker: SEACOALE
sentences.text: [aside] Peace! Stir not.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Conrad, I say!

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Here, man. I am at thy elbow.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Mass, and my elbow itched, I thought there would a
scab follow.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:
I will owe thee an answer for that. And now forward
with thy tale.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:
I will owe thee an answer for that. And now forward
with thy tale.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Stand thee close, then, under this penthouse, for it
drizzles rain, and I will, like a true drunkard, utter
all to thee.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Stand thee close, then, under this penthouse, for it
drizzles rain, and I will, like a true drunkard, utter
all to thee.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text: [aside] Some treason, masters. Yet stand close.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text: [aside] Some treason, masters. Yet stand close.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Therefore know I have earned of Don John a
thousand ducats.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Is it possible that any villainy should be so dear?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Thou shouldst rather ask if it were possible any
villainy should be so rich. For when rich villains have
need of poor ones, poor ones may make what price they
will.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Thou shouldst rather ask if it were possible any
villainy should be so rich. For when rich villains have
need of poor ones, poor ones may make what price they
will.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: I wonder at it.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
That shows thou art unconfirmed. Thou knowest that
the fashion of a doublet, or a hat, or a cloak, is
nothing to a man.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
That shows thou art unconfirmed. Thou knowest that
the fashion of a doublet, or a hat, or a cloak, is
nothing to a man.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Yes, it is apparel.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: I mean the fashion.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Yes, the fashion is the fashion.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Tush, I may as well say the fool’s the fool. But seest
thou not what a deformed thief this fashion is?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Tush, I may as well say the fool’s the fool. But seest
thou not what a deformed thief this fashion is?

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
[aside] I know that Deformed. He has been a vile thief
this seven year. He goes up and down like a gentleman.
I remember his name.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
[aside] I know that Deformed. He has been a vile thief
this seven year. He goes up and down like a gentleman.
I remember his name.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
[aside] I know that Deformed. He has been a vile thief
this seven year. He goes up and down like a gentleman.
I remember his name.

sentences.speaker: WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
[aside] I know that Deformed. He has been a vile thief
this seven year. He goes up and down like a gentleman.
I remember his name.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Didst thou not hear somebody?

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: No, ’twas the vane on the house.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Seest thou not, I say, what a deformed thief this
fashion is, how giddily he turns about all the hot
bloods between fourteen and five-and-thirty, sometimes
fashioning them like Pharaoh’s soldiers in the reechy
painting, sometime like god Bel’s priests in the old
church-window, sometime like the shaven Hercules in the
smirched worm-eaten tapestry, where his codpiece seems
as massy as his club?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Seest thou not, I say, what a deformed thief this
fashion is, how giddily he turns about all the hot
bloods between fourteen and five-and-thirty, sometimes
fashioning them like Pharaoh’s soldiers in the reechy
painting, sometime like god Bel’s priests in the old
church-window, sometime like the shaven Hercules in the
smirched worm-eaten tapestry, where his codpiece seems
as massy as his club?

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:
All this I see, and I see that the fashion wears out
more apparel than the man. But art not thou thyself
giddy with the fashion too, that thou hast shifted out
of thy tale into telling me of the fashion?

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:
All this I see, and I see that the fashion wears out
more apparel than the man. But art not thou thyself
giddy with the fashion too, that thou hast shifted out
of thy tale into telling me of the fashion?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Not so, neither. But know that I have tonight
wooed Margaret, the Lady Hero’s gentlewoman, by the name
of Hero. She leans me out at her mistress' chamber
window, bids me a thousand times good night. I tell this
tale vilely. I should first tell thee how the Prince,
Claudio and my master, planted and placed and possessed
by my master Don John, saw afar off in the orchard this
amiable encounter.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: And thought they Margaret was Hero?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
Two of them did, the Prince and Claudio, but the devil
my master knew she was Margaret; and partly by his
oaths, which first possessed them, partly by the dark
night, which did deceive them, but chiefly by my
villainy, which did confirm any slander that Don John
had made, away went Claudio enraged, swore he would meet
her as he was appointed next morning at the temple, and
there, before the whole congregation, shame her with
what he saw o'ernight
and send her home again without a husband.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO

and send her home again without a husband.

sentences.speaker: SECOND WATCHMAN
sentences.text: We charge you, in the Prince’s name, stand!

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Call up the right Master Constable. We have
here recovered the most dangerous piece of lechery that
ever was known in the commonwealth.

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Call up the right Master Constable. We have
here recovered the most dangerous piece of lechery that
ever was known in the commonwealth.

sentences.speaker: SECOND WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
And one Deformed is one of them. I know him; he wears
a lock.

sentences.speaker: SECOND WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
And one Deformed is one of them. I know him; he wears
a lock.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Masters, masters—

sentences.speaker: SECOND WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
[to BORACHIO] You’ll be made bring Deformed forth,
I warrant you.

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Masters, never speak, we charge you, let us obey you
go with us.

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Masters, never speak, we charge you, let us obey you
go with us.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text:
We are like to prove a goodly commodity, being taken up
of these men’s bills.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text:
A commodity in question, I warrant you.—Come,
we’ll obey you.
Exeunt
Enter HERO, MARGARET, and URSULA

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Good Ursula, wake my cousin Beatrice and desire her
to rise.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text: I will, lady.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: And bid her come hither.

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text: Well.
Exit

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Troth, I think your other rebato were better.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: No, pray thee, good Meg, I’ll wear this.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
By my troth, ’s not so good, and I warrant your cousin
will say so.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
My cousin’s a fool, and thou art another. I’ll wear
none but this.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
My cousin’s a fool, and thou art another. I’ll wear
none but this.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
I like the new tire within excellently, if the hair
were a thought browner; and your gown’s a most rare
fashion, i' faith. I saw the Duchess of Milan’s gown
that they praise so.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
I like the new tire within excellently, if the hair
were a thought browner; and your gown’s a most rare
fashion, i' faith. I saw the Duchess of Milan’s gown
that they praise so.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
I like the new tire within excellently, if the hair
were a thought browner; and your gown’s a most rare
fashion, i' faith. I saw the Duchess of Milan’s gown
that they praise so.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Oh, that exceeds, they say.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
By my troth, ’s but a nightgown in respect of yours
—cloth o' gold, and cuts, and laced with silver, set with
pearls, down sleeves, side sleeves, and skirts, round
underborne with a bluish tinsel. But for a fine, quaint,
graceful, and excellent fashion, yours is worth ten on
’t.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
God give me joy to wear it, for my heart is exceeding
heavy.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: 'Twill be heavier soon by the weight of a man.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Fie upon thee! Art not ashamed?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Fie upon thee! Art not ashamed?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Of what, lady? Of speaking honorably? Is not
marriage honorable in a beggar? Is not your lord
honorable without marriage? I think you would have me
say, “Saving your reverence, a husband.” An bad thinking
do not wrest true speaking, I’ll offend nobody. Is
there any harm in “the heavier for a husband?” None, I
think, an it be the right husband and the right wife.
Otherwise, ’tis light and not heavy. Ask my Lady Beatrice
else. Here she comes.

there any harm in “the heavier for a husband?” None, I
think, an it be the right husband and the right wife.
Otherwise, ’tis light and not heavy. Ask my Lady Beatrice
else. Here she comes.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Of what, lady? Of speaking honorably? Is not
marriage honorable in a beggar? Is not your lord
honorable without marriage? I think you would have me
say, “Saving your reverence, a husband.” An bad thinking
do not wrest true speaking, I’ll offend nobody. Is
there any harm in “the heavier for a husband?” None, I
think, an it be the right husband and the right wife.
Otherwise, ’tis light and not heavy. Ask my Lady Beatrice
else. Here she comes.

Enter BEATRICE

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Good morrow, coz.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Good morrow, sweet Hero.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Why, how now? Do you speak in the sick tune?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Why, how now? Do you speak in the sick tune?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: I am out of all other tune, methinks.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Clap ’s into “Light o' love.” That goes without a
burden. Do you sing it, and I’ll dance it.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Clap ’s into “Light o' love.” That goes without a
burden. Do you sing it, and I’ll dance it.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Ye light o' love, with your heels! Then, if your
husband have stables enough, you’ll see he shall lack no
barns.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Ye light o' love, with your heels! Then, if your
husband have stables enough, you’ll see he shall lack no
barns.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
O illegitimate construction! I scorn that with my
heels.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
O illegitimate construction! I scorn that with my
heels.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
'Tis almost five o'clock, cousin. 'Tis time you were
ready. By my troth, I am exceeding ill. Heigh-ho!

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
'Tis almost five o'clock, cousin. 'Tis time you were
ready. By my troth, I am exceeding ill. Heigh-ho!

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
'Tis almost five o'clock, cousin. 'Tis time you were
ready. By my troth, I am exceeding ill. Heigh-ho!

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
'Tis almost five o'clock, cousin. 'Tis time you were
ready. By my troth, I am exceeding ill. Heigh-ho!

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: For a hawk, a horse, or a husband?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: For the letter that begins them all, H.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Well, an you be not turned Turk, there’s no more
sailing by the star.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: What means the fool, trow?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Nothing, I; but God send everyone their heart’s
desire.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
These gloves the Count sent me, they are an
excellent perfume.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: I am stuffed, cousin. I cannot smell.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: I am stuffed, cousin. I cannot smell.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: A maid, and stuffed! There’s goodly catching of cold.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: A maid, and stuffed! There’s goodly catching of cold.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Oh, God help me, God help me! How long have
you professed apprehension?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Oh, God help me, God help me! How long have
you professed apprehension?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Even since you left it. Doth not my wit become me
rarely?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Even since you left it. Doth not my wit become me
rarely?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
It is not seen enough; you should wear it in your cap.
By my troth, I am sick.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
It is not seen enough; you should wear it in your cap.
By my troth, I am sick.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Get you some of this distilled carduus benedictus and
lay it to your heart. It is the only thing for a qualm.

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Get you some of this distilled carduus benedictus and
lay it to your heart. It is the only thing for a qualm.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: There thou prick’st her with a thistle.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Benedictus! Why benedictus? You have some moral in
this benedictus?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Benedictus! Why benedictus? You have some moral in
this benedictus?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Benedictus! Why benedictus? You have some moral in
this benedictus?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text:
Moral! No, by my troth, I have no moral meaning. I
meant plain holy thistle. You may think perchance that I
think you are in love. Nay, by 'r Lady, I am not such a
fool to think what I list, nor I list not to think what
I can, nor indeed I cannot think, if I would think my
heart out of thinking, that you are in love or that you
will be in love or that you can be in love. Yet Benedick
was such another, and now is he become a man. He swore
he would never marry, and yet now, in despite of his
heart, he eats his meat without grudging. And how you
may be converted I know not, but methinks you look with
your eyes as other women do.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: What pace is this that thy tongue keeps?

sentences.speaker: MARGARET
sentences.text: Not a false gallop.
Enter URSULA

sentences.speaker: URSULA
sentences.text:
Madam, withdraw: the Prince, the Count,
Signor Benedick, Don John, and all the gallants of the
town are come to fetch you to church.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: Help to dress me, good coz, good Meg, good Ursula.
Exeunt
Enter LEONATO with DOGBERRY and VERGES

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: What would you with me, honest neighbor?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Marry, sir, I would have some confidence with you
that decerns you nearly.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Brief, I pray you, for you see it is a busy time with
me.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Marry, this it is, sir.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Yes, in truth it is, sir.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: What is it, my good friends?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Goodman Verges, sir, speaks a little off the matter. An
old man, sir, and his wits are not so blunt as, God
help, I would desire they were, but, in faith, honest as
the skin between his brows.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Goodman Verges, sir, speaks a little off the matter. An
old man, sir, and his wits are not so blunt as, God
help, I would desire they were, but, in faith, honest as
the skin between his brows.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Goodman Verges, sir, speaks a little off the matter. An
old man, sir, and his wits are not so blunt as, God
help, I would desire they were, but, in faith, honest as
the skin between his brows.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text:
Yes, I thank God I am as honest as any man living that
is an old man and no honester than I.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Comparisons are odorous. Palabras , neighbor Verges.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Comparisons are odorous. Palabras , neighbor Verges.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Neighbors, you are tedious.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
It pleases your Worship to say so, but we are the poor
duke’s officers. But truly, for mine own part, if I were
as tedious as a king, I could find it in my heart to
bestow it all of your worship.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
It pleases your Worship to say so, but we are the poor
duke’s officers. But truly, for mine own part, if I were
as tedious as a king, I could find it in my heart to
bestow it all of your worship.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: All thy tediousness on me, ah?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Yea, an ’twere a thousand pound more than ’tis, for I
hear as good exclamation on your Worship as of any man
in the city, and though I be but a poor man, I am glad
to hear it.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: And so am I.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: I would fain know what you have to say.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text:
Marry, sir, our watch tonight, excepting your
Worship’s presence, ha' ta'en a couple of as arrant
knaves as any in Messina.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
A good old man, sir. He will be talking. As they say,
“When the age is in, the wit is out.” God help us, it is
a world to see! Well said, i' faith, neighbor Verges.
—Well, God’s a good man. An two men ride of a horse, one
must ride behind. An honest soul, i' faith, sir, by my
troth he is, as ever broke bread, but God is to be
worshipped, all men are not alike, alas, good neighbor!

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Indeed, neighbor, he comes too short of you.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Gifts that God gives.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: I must leave you.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
One word, sir. Our watch, sir, have indeed
comprehended two aspicious persons, and we would have
them this morning examined before your worship.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
One word, sir. Our watch, sir, have indeed
comprehended two aspicious persons, and we would have
them this morning examined before your worship.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Take their examination yourself and bring it me. I am
now in great haste, as it may appear unto you.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Take their examination yourself and bring it me. I am
now in great haste, as it may appear unto you.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: It shall be suffigance.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Drink some wine ere you go. Fare you well.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Drink some wine ere you go. Fare you well.
Enter a MESSENGER

sentences.speaker: MESSENGER
sentences.text:
My lord, they stay for you to give your daughter to
her husband.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: I’ll wait upon them. I am ready.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: I’ll wait upon them. I am ready.
Exeunt LEONATO and MESSENGER

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Go, good partner, go, get you to Francis Seacole. Bid
him bring his pen and inkhorn to the jail. We are now
to examination these men.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Go, good partner, go, get you to Francis Seacole. Bid
him bring his pen and inkhorn to the jail. We are now
to examination these men.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Go, good partner, go, get you to Francis Seacole. Bid
him bring his pen and inkhorn to the jail. We are now
to examination these men.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Go, good partner, go, get you to Francis Seacole. Bid
him bring his pen and inkhorn to the jail. We are now
to examination these men.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: And we must do it wisely.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
We will spare for no wit, I warrant you. Here’s that
shall drive some of them to a noncome. Only get the
learned writer to set down our excommunication and meet
me at the jail.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:

learned writer to set down our excommunication and meet
me at the jail.

sentences.text:

Exeunt
Enter DON PEDRO, DON JOHN, LEONATO, FRIAR FRANCIS, CLAUDIO, BENEDICK, HERO, BEATRICE, and Attendants

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Come, Friar Francis, be brief, only to the plain form
of marriage, and you shall recount their particular
duties afterwards.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Come, Friar Francis, be brief, only to the plain form
of marriage, and you shall recount their particular
duties afterwards.

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text:
[to CLAUDIO] You come hither, my lord, to marry this
lady?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: No.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: To be married to her.—Friar, you come to marry her.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: To be married to her.—Friar, you come to marry her.

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text: Lady, you come hither to be married to this count?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: I do.

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text:
If either of you know any inward impediment why
you should not be conjoined, charge you on your souls to
utter it.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Know you any, Hero?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: None, my lord.

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text: Know you any, count?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: I dare make his answer, none.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
O, what men dare do! What men may do! What men
daily do, not knowing what they do!

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
O, what men dare do! What men may do! What men
daily do, not knowing what they do!

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
O, what men dare do! What men may do! What men
daily do, not knowing what they do!

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
How now, interjections? Why, then, some be of
laughing, as, ah, ha, he!

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
How now, interjections? Why, then, some be of
laughing, as, ah, ha, he!

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Stand thee by, Friar.—Father, by your leave, Will you
with free and unconstrainèd soul
Give me this maid, your daughter?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: As freely, son, as God did give her me.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
And what have I to give you back whose worth May
counterpoise this rich and precious gift?

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Nothing, unless you render her again.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Sweet Prince, you learn me noble thankfulness.—
There, Leonato, take her back again.
Give not this rotten orange to your friend.
She’s but the sign and semblance of her honor.
Behold how like a maid she blushes here!
Oh, what authority and show of truth
Can cunning sin cover itself withal!
Comes not that blood as modest evidence
To witness simple virtue? Would you not swear,
All you that see her, that she were a maid
By these exterior shows? But she is none.
She knows the heat of a luxurious bed.
Her blush is guiltiness, not modesty.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: What do you mean, my lord?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Not to be married,
Not to knit my soul to an approvèd wanton.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Not to be married,
Not to knit my soul to an approvèd wanton.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Dear my lord, if you in your own proof
Have vanquished the resistance of her youth
And made defeat of her virginity—

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
I know what you would say: if I have known her,
You will say she did embrace me as a husband,
And so extenuate the forehand sin. No, Leonato,
I never tempted her with word too large
But, as a brother to his sister, showed
Bashful sincerity and comely love.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: And seemed I ever otherwise to you?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Out on thee, seeming! I will write against it.
You seem to me as Dian in her orb,
As chaste as is the bud ere it be blown.
But you are more intemperate in your blood
Than Venus, or those pampered animals
That rage in savage sensuality.

sentences.speaker: HERO

sentences.speaker: LEONATO

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
What should I speak?
I stand dishonored, that have gone about
To link my dear friend to a common stale.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK

sentences.speaker: HERO

Is this face Hero’s? Are our eyes our own?

That you have in her, bid her answer truly.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: I charge thee do so, as thou art my child.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Oh, God defend me! how am I beset!—
What kind of catechizing call you this?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Oh, God defend me! how am I beset!—
What kind of catechizing call you this?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: To make you answer truly to your name.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Is it not Hero? Who can blot that name
With any just reproach?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
Is it not Hero? Who can blot that name
With any just reproach?

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
Marry, that can Hero!
Hero itself can blot out Hero’s virtue.
What man was he talked with you yesternight
Out at your window betwixt twelve and one?
Now, if you are a maid, answer to this.

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text: I talked with no man at that hour, my lord.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text:
Why, then are you no maiden. —Leonato,
I am sorry you must hear. Upon mine honor,
Myself, my brother, and this grievèd count
Did see her, hear her, at that hour last night
Talk with a ruffian at her chamber window
Who hath indeed, most like a liberal villain,
Confessed the vile encounters they have had
A thousand times in secret.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Fie, fie, they are not to be named, my lord,
Not to be spoke of!
There is not chastity enough in language,
Without offense, to utter them. —Thus, pretty lady,
I am sorry for thy much misgovernment.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text:
O Hero, what a Hero hadst thou been
If half thy outward graces had been placed
About thy thoughts and counsels of thy heart!
But fare thee well, most foul, most fair! Farewell,
Thou pure impiety and impious purity.
For thee I’ll lock up all the gates of love,
And on my eyelids shall conjecture hang,
To turn all beauty into thoughts of harm,
And never shall it more be gracious.

sentences.text: Hath no man’s dagger here a point for me?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Hath no man’s dagger here a point for me?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Why, how now, cousin! wherefore sink you down?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Why, how now, cousin! wherefore sink you down?

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Come, let us go. These things, come thus to light,
Smother her spirits up.

sentences.speaker: DON JOHN
sentences.text:
Come, let us go. These things, come thus to light,
Smother her spirits up.
Exeunt DON PEDRO, DON JOHN, and CLAUDIO

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: How doth the lady?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Dead, I think.—Help, uncle!—
Hero, why, Hero! Uncle! Signor Benedick! Friar!

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE

Hero, why, Hero! Uncle! Signor Benedick! Friar!

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
O Fate! Take not away thy heavy hand!
Death is the fairest cover for her shame
That may be wished for.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
O Fate! Take not away thy heavy hand!
Death is the fairest cover for her shame
That may be wished for.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: How now, cousin Hero!

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: How now, cousin Hero!

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text: [to HERO] Have comfort, lady.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: [to HERO] Dost thou look up?

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text: Yea, wherefore should she not?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Wherefore! Why, doth not every earthly thing
Cry shame upon her? Could she here deny
The story that is printed in her blood?—
Do not live, Hero, do not ope thine eyes,
For, did I think thou wouldst not quickly die,
Thought I thy spirits were stronger than thy shames,
Myself would, on the rearward of reproaches,
Strike at thy life. Grieved I I had but one?
Chid I for that at frugal Nature’s frame?
O, one too much by thee! Why had I one?
Why ever wast thou lovely in my eyes?
Why had I not with charitable hand
Took up a beggar’s issue at my gates,
Who, smirchèd thus, and mired with infamy,
I might have said, “No part of it is mine;
This shame derives itself from unknown loins”?
But mine, and mine I loved, and mine I praised,
And mine that I was proud on, mine so much
That I myself was to myself not mine,
Valuing of her– why, she, O she is fall'n
Into a pit of ink, that the wide sea
Hath drops too few to wash her clean again
And salt too little which may season give
To her foul tainted flesh!

Valuing of her– why, she, O she is fall'n
Into a pit of ink, that the wide sea
Hath drops too few to wash her clean again
And salt too little which may season give
To her foul tainted flesh!

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Wherefore! Why, doth not every earthly thing
Cry shame upon her? Could she here deny
The story that is printed in her blood?—
Do not live, Hero, do not ope thine eyes,
For, did I think thou wouldst not quickly die,
Thought I thy spirits were stronger than thy shames,
Myself would, on the rearward of reproaches,
Strike at thy life. Grieved I I had but one?
Chid I for that at frugal Nature’s frame?
O, one too much by thee! Why had I one?
Why ever wast thou lovely in my eyes?
Why had I not with charitable hand
Took up a beggar’s issue at my gates,
Who, smirchèd thus, and mired with infamy,
I might have said, “No part of it is mine;
This shame derives itself from unknown loins”?
But mine, and mine I loved, and mine I praised,
And mine that I was proud on, mine so much
That I myself was to myself not mine,
Valuing of her– why, she, O she is fall'n
Into a pit of ink, that the wide sea
Hath drops too few to wash her clean again
And salt too little which may season give
To her foul tainted flesh!

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Sir, sir, be patient.
For my part, I am so attired in wonder
I know not what to say.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:

I know not what to say.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Oh, on my soul, my cousin is belied!

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Lady, were you her bedfellow last night?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
No, truly not, although until last night
I have this twelvemonth been her bedfellow.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:

I have this twelvemonth been her bedfellow.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Confirmed, confirmed! Oh, that is stronger made
Which was before barred up with ribs of iron!
Would the two princes lie and Claudio lie,
Who loved her so that, speaking of her foulness,
Washed it with tears? Hence from her. Let her die.

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text:
Hear me a little,
For I have only silent been so long,
And given way unto this course of fortune,
By noting of the lady. I have marked
A thousand blushing apparitions
To start into her face, a thousand innocent shames
In angel whiteness beat away those blushes,
And in her eye there hath appeared a fire
To burn the errors that these princes hold
Against her maiden truth. Call me a fool,
Trust not my reading nor my observations,
Which with experimental seal doth warrant
The tenor of my book; trust not my age,
My reverence, calling, nor divinity,
If this sweet lady lie not guiltless here
Under some biting error.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Friar, it cannot be.
Thou seest that all the grace that she hath left
Is that she will not add to her damnation
A sin of perjury. She not denies it.
Why seek’st thou then to cover with excuse
That which appears in proper nakedness?

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text: Lady, what man is he you are accused of?

sentences.speaker: HERO
sentences.text:
They know that do accuse me. I know none.
If I know more of any man alive
Than that which maiden modesty doth warrant,
Let all my sins lack mercy! —O my father,
Prove you that any man with me conversed
At hours unmeet, or that I yesternight
Maintained the change of words with any creature,
Refuse me, hate me, torture me to death!

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text: There is some strange misprision in the princes.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Two of them have the very bent of honor,
And if their wisdoms be misled in this,
The practice of it lives in John the Bastard,
Whose spirits toil in frame of villainies.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Two of them have the very bent of honor,
And if their wisdoms be misled in this,

Whose spirits toil in frame of villainies.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
I know not. If they speak but truth of her,
These hands shall tear her; if they wrong her honor,
The proudest of them shall well hear of it.
Time hath not yet so dried this blood of mine
Nor age so eat up my invention
Nor fortune made such havoc of my means
Nor my bad life reft me so much of friends
But they shall find, awaked in such a kind,
Both strength of limb and policy of mind,
Ability in means and choice of friends,
To quit me of them throughly.

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text:
Pause awhile,
And let my counsel sway you in this case.
Your daughter here the princes left for dead.
Let her awhile be secretly kept in
And publish it that she is dead indeed.
Maintain a mourning ostentation,
And on your family’s old monument
Hang mournful epitaphs and do all rites
That appertain unto a burial.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: What shall become of this? What will this do?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: What shall become of this? What will this do?

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text:
Marry, this, well carried, shall on her behalf
Change slander to remorse. That is some good.
But not for that dream I on this strange course,
But on this travail look for greater birth.
She, dying, as it must so be maintained,
Upon the instant that she was accused,
Shall be lamented, pitied and excused
Of every hearer. For it so falls out
That what we have we prize not to the worth
Whiles we enjoy it, but being lacked and lost,
Why then we rack the value, then we find
The virtue that possession would not show us
Whiles it was ours. So will it fare with Claudio.
When he shall hear she died upon his words,
The idea of her life shall sweetly creep
Into his study of imagination,
And every lovely organ of her life
Shall come apparelled in more precious habit,
More moving, delicate and full of life,
Into the eye and prospect of his soul
Than when she lived indeed. Then shall he mourn,
If ever love had interest in his liver,
And wish he had not so accused her,
No, though he thought his accusation true.
Let this be so, and doubt not but success
Will fashion the event in better shape
Than I can lay it down in likelihood.
But if all aim but this be leveled false,
The supposition of the lady's death
Will quench the wonder of her infamy.
And if it sort not well, you may conceal her,
As best befits her wounded reputation,
In some reclusive and religious life,
Out of all eyes, tongues, minds, and injuries.

Marry, this, well carried, shall on her behalf
Change slander to remorse. That is some good.
But not for that dream I on this strange course,
But on this travail look for greater birth.
She, dying, as it must so be maintained,
Upon the instant that she was accused,
Shall be lamented, pitied and excused
Of every hearer. For it so falls out
That what we have we prize not to the worth
Whiles we enjoy it, but being lacked and lost,
Why then we rack the value, then we find
The virtue that possession would not show us
Whiles it was ours. So will it fare with Claudio.
When he shall hear she died upon his words,
The idea of her life shall sweetly creep
Into his study of imagination,
And every lovely organ of her life
Shall come apparelled in more precious habit,
More moving, delicate and full of life,
Into the eye and prospect of his soul
Than when she lived indeed. Then shall he mourn,
If ever love had interest in his liver,
And wish he had not so accused her,
No, though he thought his accusation true.
Let this be so, and doubt not but success
Will fashion the event in better shape
Than I can lay it down in likelihood.
But if all aim but this be leveled false,
The supposition of the lady's death
Will quench the wonder of her infamy.
And if it sort not well, you may conceal her,
As best befits her wounded reputation,
In some reclusive and religious life,
Out of all eyes, tongues, minds, and injuries.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Signior Leonato, let the friar advise you.
And though you know my inwardness and love
Is very much unto the Prince and Claudio,
Yet, by mine honor, I will deal in this
As secretly and justly as your soul
Should with your body.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Signior Leonato, let the friar advise you.
And though you know my inwardness and love
Is very much unto the Prince and Claudio,
Yet, by mine honor, I will deal in this
As secretly and justly as your soul
Should with your body.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Being that I flow in grief,
The smallest twine may lead me.

sentences.speaker: FRIAR FRANCIS
sentences.text:
'Tis well consented. Presently away,
For to strange sores strangely they strain the cure.—
Come, lady, die to live. This wedding day
Perhaps is but prolonged. Have patience and endure.

Exeunt all but BENEDICK and BEATRICE

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Lady Beatrice, have you wept all this while?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Yea, and I will weep a while longer.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: I will not desire that.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: You have no reason. I do it freely.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: You have no reason. I do it freely.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Surely I do believe your fair cousin is wronged.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Ah, how much might the man deserve of me that
would right her!

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Is there any way to show such friendship?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: A very even way, but no such friend.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: May a man do it?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: It is a man’s office, but not yours.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I do love nothing in the world so well as you. Is not
that strange?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I do love nothing in the world so well as you. Is not
that strange?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
As strange as the thing I know not. It were as possible
for me to say I loved nothing so well as you, but
believe me not, and yet I lie not, I confess nothing,
nor I deny nothing. I am sorry for my cousin.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:

believe me not, and yet I lie not, I confess nothing,
nor I deny nothing. I am sorry for my cousin.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: By my sword, Beatrice, thou lovest me.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Do not swear, and eat it.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Do not swear, and eat it.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
I will swear by it that you love me, and I will make
him eat it that says I love not you.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Will you not eat your word?

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
With no sauce that can be devised to it. I protest I
love thee.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
With no sauce that can be devised to it. I protest I
love thee.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Why then, God forgive me.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: What offense, sweet Beatrice?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
You have stayed me in a happy hour. I was about to
protest
I loved you.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
You have stayed me in a happy hour. I was about to
protest
I loved you.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: And do it with all thy heart.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I love you with so much of my heart that none is left
to protest.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Come, bid me do anything for thee.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Kill Claudio.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Ha! Not for the wide world.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Ha! Not for the wide world.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: You kill me to deny it. Farewell.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: You kill me to deny it. Farewell.
BEATRICE begins to exit

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Tarry, sweet Beatrice.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I am gone, though I am here. There is no love in you.
Nay, I pray you let me go.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I am gone, though I am here. There is no love in you.
Nay, I pray you let me go.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
I am gone, though I am here. There is no love in you.
Nay, I pray you let me go.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Beatrice—

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: In faith, I will go.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: We’ll be friends first.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
You dare easier be friends with me than fight with
mine enemy.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Is Claudio thine enemy?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Is he not approved in the height a villain, that
hath slandered, scorned, dishonored my kinswoman? Oh,
that I were a man! What, bear her in hand until they
come to take hands and then, with public accusation,
uncovered slander, unmitigated rancor —O God, that I were
a man! I would eat his heart in the marketplace.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:

that I were a man! What, bear her in hand until they

a man! I would eat his heart in the marketplace.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Hear me, Beatrice—

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Talk with a man out at a window! A proper saying!

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Talk with a man out at a window! A proper saying!

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Nay, but Beatrice—

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Sweet Hero, she is wronged, she is slandered, she
is undone.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Sweet Hero, she is wronged, she is slandered, she
is undone.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Beat—

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Princes and counties! Surely, a princely testimony, a
goodly count, Count Comfect, a sweet gallant, surely!
Oh, that I were a man for his sake! Or that I had any
friend would be a man for my sake! But manhood is melted
into curtsies, valor into compliment, and men are only
turned into tongue, and trim ones too. He is now as
valiant as Hercules that only tells a lie and swears it.
I cannot be a man with wishing, therefore I will die a
woman with grieving.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text:
Princes and counties! Surely, a princely testimony, a
goodly count, Count Comfect, a sweet gallant, surely!
Oh, that I were a man for his sake! Or that I had any
friend would be a man for my sake! But manhood is melted
into curtsies, valor into compliment, and men are only
turned into tongue, and trim ones too. He is now as
valiant as Hercules that only tells a lie and swears it.
I cannot be a man with wishing, therefore I will die a
woman with grieving.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Tarry, good Beatrice. By this hand, I love thee.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text: Tarry, good Beatrice. By this hand, I love thee.

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Use it for my love some other way than swearing by it.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Think you in your soul the Count Claudio hath
wronged Hero?

sentences.speaker: BEATRICE
sentences.text: Yea, as sure as I have a thought or a soul.

sentences.speaker: BENEDICK
sentences.text:
Enough, I am engaged. I will challenge him. I will
kiss your hand, and so I leave you. By this hand,
Claudio shall render me a dear account. As you hear of
me, so think of me. Go comfort your cousin. I must say
she is dead, and so, farewell.

Enter DOGBERRY, VERGES, and SEXTON, in gowns; and the Watch, with CONRAD and BORACHIO

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Is our whole dissembly appeared?

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Oh, a stool and a cushion for the Sexton.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Oh, a stool and a cushion for the Sexton.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Oh, a stool and a cushion for the Sexton.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Oh, a stool and a cushion for the Sexton.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Oh, a stool and a cushion for the Sexton.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text: Which be the malefactors?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Marry, that am I and my partner.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text:
Nay, that’s certain; we have the exhibition to
examine.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text:
But which are the offenders that are to be examined?
Let
them come before Master Constable.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text:
But which are the offenders that are to be examined?
Let
them come before Master Constable.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Yea, marry, let them come before me.
BORACHIO and CONRAD come forward

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: What is your name, friend?

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Borachio.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Pray, write down, “Borachio.”—Yours, sirrah?

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: I am a gentleman, sir, and my name is Conrad.

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: I am a gentleman, sir, and my name is Conrad.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Write down “Master Gentleman Conrad.”—Masters, do you
serve God?

sentences.speaker: CONRAD BORACHIO
sentences.text: Yea, sir, we hope.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Write down that they hope they serve God; and write
God first, for God defend but God should go before
such villains! —Masters, it is proved already that you
are little better than false knaves, and it will go near
to be thought so shortly. How answer you for
yourselves?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY

sentences.text: Marry, sir, we say we are none.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
A marvelous witty fellow, I assure you, but I will go
about with him.—Come you hither, sirrah, a word in your
ear. Sir, I say to you it is thought you are false
knaves.

sentences.text:
A marvelous witty fellow, I assure you, but I will go

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Sir, I say to you we are none.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Well, stand aside.—'Fore God, they are both in a ale.
Have you writ down that they are none?

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Well, stand aside.—'Fore God, they are both in a ale.
Have you writ down that they are none?

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text:
Master Constable, you go not the way to examine.
You must call forth the watch that are their accusers.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text:
Master Constable, you go not the way to examine.
You must call forth the watch that are their accusers.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Yea, marry, that’s the eftest way.—Let the watch
come forth. Masters, I charge you in the Prince’s name,
accuse these men.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Yea, marry, that’s the eftest way.—Let the watch
come forth. Masters, I charge you in the Prince’s name,
accuse these men.

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
This man said, sir, that Don John, the Prince’s
brother, was a villain.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Write down Prince John a villain. Why, this is flat
perjury, to call a prince’s brother villain.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Write down Prince John a villain. Why, this is flat
perjury, to call a prince’s brother villain.

sentences.speaker: BORACHIO
sentences.text: Master Constable—

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Pray thee, fellow, peace. I do not like thy look, I
promise thee.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Pray thee, fellow, peace. I do not like thy look, I
promise thee.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text: [to Watch] What heard you him say else?

sentences.speaker: SECOND WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
Marry, that he had received a thousand ducats of Don
John for accusing the Lady Hero wrongfully.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Flat burglary as ever was committed.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Yea, by Mass, that it is.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text: What else, fellow?

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text:
And that Count Claudio did mean upon his words
to disgrace Hero before the whole assembly, and not
marry her.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
[to BORACHIO] O villain! Thou wilt be condemned
into everlasting redemption for this.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
[to BORACHIO] O villain! Thou wilt be condemned
into everlasting redemption for this.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text: What else?

sentences.speaker: FIRST WATCHMAN
sentences.text: This is all.

sentences.speaker: SEXTON
sentences.text:
And this is more, masters, than you can deny. Prince
John is this morning secretly stolen away. Hero was in
this manner accused, in this very manner refused, and
upon the grief of this, suddenly died. —Master Constable,
let these men be bound and brought to Leonato’s. I will
go before and show him their examination.

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text: Come, let them be opinioned.

sentences.speaker: VERGES
sentences.text: Let them be in the hands—

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Off, coxcomb!

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
God’s my life, where’s the Sexton? Let him write down
the
Prince’s officer “coxcomb.” Come, bind them.—Thou
naughty varlet!

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
God’s my life, where’s the Sexton? Let him write down
the
Prince’s officer “coxcomb.” Come, bind them.—Thou
naughty varlet!

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Away! You are an ass, you are an ass!

sentences.speaker: CONRAD
sentences.text: Away! You are an ass, you are an ass!

sentences.speaker: DOGBERRY
sentences.text:
Dost thou not suspect my place? Dost thou not suspect
my years? Oh, that he were here to write me down an ass!
But masters, remember that I am an ass, though it be
not written down, yet forget not that I am an ass. —No,
thou villain, thou art full of piety, as shall be proved
upon thee by good witness. I am a wise fellow and,
which is more, an officer and, which is more, a
householder and, which is more, as pretty a piece of
flesh as any is in Messina, and one that knows the law,
go to, and a rich fellow enough, go to, and a fellow
that hath had losses, and one that hath two gowns and
everything handsome about him. —Bring him away.—Oh, that
I had been writ down an ass!

Enter LEONATO and ANTONIO

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
If you go on thus, you will kill yourself,
And ’tis not wisdom thus to second grief
Against yourself.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
If you go on thus, you will kill yourself,
And ’tis not wisdom thus to second grief
Against yourself.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
I pray thee, cease thy counsel,
Which falls into mine ears as profitless
As water in a sieve. Give not me counsel,
Nor let no comforter delight mine ear
But such a one whose wrongs do suit with mine.
Bring me a father that so loved his child,
Whose joy of her is overwhelmed like mine,
And bid him speak of patience.
Measure his woe the length and breadth of mine,
And let it answer every strain for strain,
As thus for thus and such a grief for such,
In every lineament, branch, shape, and form.
If such a one will smile and stroke his beard,
Bid sorrow wag, cry “hem” when he should groan,
Patch grief with proverbs, make misfortune drunk
With candle-wasters, bring him yet to me
And I of him will gather patience.
But there is no such man. For, brother, men
Can counsel and speak comfort to that grief
Which they themselves not feel, but, tasting it,
Their counsel turns to passion which before
Would give preceptial med'cine to rage,
Fetter strong madness in a silken thread,
Charm ache with air, and agony with words.
No, no, 'tis all men's office to speak patience
To those that wring under the load of sorrow,
But no man's virtue nor sufficiency
To be so moral when he shall endure
The like himself. Therefore give me no counsel.
My griefs cry louder than advertisement.

Charm ache with air, and agony with words.
No, no, 'tis all men's office to speak patience
To those that wring under the load of sorrow,
But no man's virtue nor sufficiency
To be so moral when he shall endure
The like himself. Therefore give me no counsel.
My griefs cry louder than advertisement.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
I pray thee, cease thy counsel,
Which falls into mine ears as profitless
As water in a sieve. Give not me counsel,
Nor let no comforter delight mine ear
But such a one whose wrongs do suit with mine.
Bring me a father that so loved his child,
Whose joy of her is overwhelmed like mine,
And bid him speak of patience.
Measure his woe the length and breadth of mine,
And let it answer every strain for strain,
As thus for thus and such a grief for such,
In every lineament, branch, shape, and form.
If such a one will smile and stroke his beard,
Bid sorrow wag, cry “hem” when he should groan,
Patch grief with proverbs, make misfortune drunk
With candle-wasters, bring him yet to me
And I of him will gather patience.
But there is no such man. For, brother, men
Can counsel and speak comfort to that grief
Which they themselves not feel, but, tasting it,
Their counsel turns to passion which before
Would give preceptial med'cine to rage,
Fetter strong madness in a silken thread,
Charm ache with air, and agony with words.
No, no, 'tis all men's office to speak patience
To those that wring under the load of sorrow,
But no man's virtue nor sufficiency
To be so moral when he shall endure
The like himself. Therefore give me no counsel.
My griefs cry louder than advertisement.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: Therein do men from children nothing differ.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
I pray thee, peace. I will be flesh and blood,
For there was never yet philosopher
That could endure the toothache patiently,
However they have writ the style of gods
And made a push at chance and sufferance.

Yet bend not all the harm upon yourself.
Make those that do offend you suffer too.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
There thou speak’st reason. Nay, I will do so.
My soul doth tell me Hero is belied,
And that shall Claudio know; so shall the Prince
And all of them that thus dishonor her.

Enter DON PEDRO and CLAUDIO

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text: Here comes the Prince and Claudio hastily.

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Good e'en, good e'en.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Good day to both of you.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text: Hear you, my lords—

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: We have some haste, Leonato.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Some haste, my lord! Well, fare you well, my lord.
Are you so hasty now? Well, all is one.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:

sentences.speaker: DON PEDRO
sentences.text: Nay, do not quarrel with us, good old man.

sentences.speaker: ANTONIO
sentences.text:
If he could right himself with quarreling,
Some of us would lie low.

sentences.speaker: CLAUDIO
sentences.text: Who wrongs him?

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:
Marry, thou dost wrong me, thou dissembler, thou.
Nay, never lay thy hand upon thy sword.
I fear thee not.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
sentences.text:

Nay, never lay thy hand upon thy sword.
I fear thee not.

sentences.speaker: LEONATO
